Therefore: check now to keep the pot small and preserve chips.

I have completed a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5) on a board of 9h-4s-2h-3c-Ac. On the turn, Minni checked back, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a hand looking to get to showdown cheaply. The Ace on the river is a perfect card for me to lead into her. I likely have the best hand here, as 5-6 (the only higher straight) or sets are less likely given the turn check. I will bet for value, targeting her 9x, Ax, or pocket pairs that might call a reasonably sized bet thinking I am representing the Ace that just hit. I'll bet 750 into the 1100 pot.

I checked the river as planned and Flash fired 750 into the 1100 pot, making the pot 1850 and presenting me a 750 call for ~40.5% pot odds. I hold K♠7♣ with no pair, no backdoor outs, and very little showdown value. Flash’s line (flat preflop, call/call on flop and turn, then a substantial river bet) is much more consistent with a made hand or a rivered Ace (Ax), pocket pairs (99/44/22/33), or a 9x — all of which beat K7. My hand’s equity versus that range is far below the ~40% needed to justify a call. Raising as a bluff would be thin and I don’t block Ax combos, so it’s too risky and easy to exploit. I will fold and wait for a better, more favorable spot in position.

Position: Button with QJ offsuit is a premium hand. The big blind (Minni) has a large stack at 20,975, but the small blind (Flash) is shorter at 7,300, which suggests potential chip pressure. 

From the button, I should leverage positional advantage with a strong broadway hand. QJ can make straights, pairs, and is ahead of most weaker holdings. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard aggression that:
1. Builds the pot in a favorable position
2. Defines my hand strength and narrows the field
3. Takes down chips if both opponents fold
4. Gives me good odds if called and flop texture is favorable

This is a profitable play with positive expected value from the button with this hand quality.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 4c, which is a very weak hand. Claude, on the button, has raised to 300. Calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a sub-par holding, and I would also be vulnerable to a squeeze from Minni in the Big Blind. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand.

I'm in the Big Blind with T3o facing a standard Button open to 300. The pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total), which gives ~30.8% pot odds — good enough to defend vs a wide button opening range. T3o is marginal but has some showdown value (can pair a ten) and occasional backdoor equity; with deep stacks it's correct to see a flop rather than folding or thin 3-betting a poorly playable hand OOP. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed only with a ten/strong draw or a favorable board, and fold to large aggression on dry unfavorable flops.

I’m OOP in the BB with T3o on a Q-6-2 rainbow flop. I have no pair, no meaningful backdoors (no feasible flush/straight), only six clean outs to make a pair (≈24% to hit a Ten or a 3 by the river). This is a dry, unfavorable board and the Button will c-bet a high frequency, so the right line is pot control and realization of equity rather than bluffing or barreling OOP with nothing. I’ll check to keep the pot small and fold to large aggression; I will consider calling a small c-bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot) to realize my ~24% chance to pair, but fold to bigger sizing. Checking also preserves stack depth for future hands and avoids bluff-raising with no blockers or equity.

C-betting top pair with a strong Jack kicker on a dry flop after my opponent checks. The low flop (2, 6) and my pre-flop raise initiative mean I should build the pot for value. A bet of 250 (roughly 38% of pot) is standard and gets value from weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable. Minni's check indicates weakness - likely drawing hands, weak pairs, or undercards.

Call 250. Claude’s flop bet is small (250 into 900 ≈ 28% of the pot) which gives good pot odds to see the turn. I have T3o with six clean outs (three tens + three threes) — ~24% to improve to a pair by the river. Calling 250 to win a pot of 1,150 requires ~21.7% equity, so a flat call is slightly +EV. I’m out of position and have no backdoor draws or useful blockers, so I won’t bluff-raise; the correct line is to realize equity cheaply. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair I can proceed for value or check-call; if I miss and face sizable turn aggression (large bet/raise, roughly >~1/2 pot) I will fold, but I’ll continue vs small turn bets to realize equity given the favorable pot odds. Stack depth makes this a cheap, correct defend.

I hold Td3c on a 2d-6h-Qh-2c board and missed — I have only 6 outs (three tens + three threes). With one card to come my chance to improve is ~6/46 ≈ 13%. The pot is 1,150 and there is currently no bet to call, so the least-risky, +EV line is to control the pot size and let Claude act. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity versus his continuing range and would commit chips with only ~13% equity. I called the flop because two cards to come gave ~24% equity vs the price; on the turn that drops to ~13%, so I should not build the pot. Plan: check and fold to large aggression; call only very small turn bets that give correct pot odds (roughly ≤≈170 chips to call), and if Claude checks back I will re-evaluate the river (value bet if I pair, consider a river bluff only if board/sizing/read give real fold equity). Action: check.

I have top pair, top kicker (QJ on a 2d 6h Qh 2c board). Minni's passive line—check-calling the flop and checking the turn—indicates a medium-strength hand like a middle pair (66), undercards, or a marginal draw. The turn deuce is a relatively dry card that rarely improves her hand significantly. 

Continuation betting here for value is essential:
1. Builds the pot when I'm ahead (likely the case)
2. Extracts value from weaker holdings she might call with
3. Maintains aggressive pressure on her passive line
4. Prevents free cards to potential draws

A 600-chip bet (roughly 52% of the 1150 pot) is standard turn value sizing—large enough to build the pot, but not so large that it folds out the weaker hands I want to extract value from. This bet should get calls from 66, Ax holdings, and small pairs.

I have T♦3♣ on 2♦-6♥-Q♥-2♣ and missed the turn. I only have 3 tens + 3 threes = 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river). Claude's turn bet is 600 into a 1,750 pot; calling 600 would make the pot 2,350 so I need roughly 600/2350 ≈ 25.5% equity to breakeven. My ~13% equity is far below that, so calling is -EV. A raise would be a pure bluff OOP with no meaningful blockers and a low chance to fold out enough of Claude's continuing range, so it’s not attractive. I defended the flop because two cards to come gave ~24% equity vs the price, but on the turn that drops to ~13% — fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play in position post-flop against the SB or BB. I have been opening to 2.5x-3x in previous hands, so 300 is consistent with my established betting patterns.

I’m in the small blind with 5♣7♦ facing a button raise to 300. This is an offsuit, low, poorly-connected hand with very weak postflop playability and no useful blockers. Calling 250 into the 450 pot means I need ~35.7% equity to break even (250/(450+250)). Versus a button opening range I don’t expect 5♣7♦ to have that equity, and if the big blind comes along my equity drops further while I’ll be out of position for the whole hand. 3-betting as a bluff here is too thin and unnecessary given my deep stack and better spots to pick later. Fold and preserve chips.

With pocket 8s in the big blind heads-up against Flash, I'm in a strong position. Flash raised to 300 from the button with a short 7k stack, which is a relatively small raise. My pocket eights are a solid hand in heads-up play (~top 20% of starting hands). 

Key factors:
1. Pocket 8s is ahead of most of Flash's range in this spot
2. Flash's short stack (7k) and small raise suggest he may be taking a wider range
3. I should leverage my hand strength and position advantage
4. Raising applies pressure on his short stack and forces a meaningful decision
5. A raise can either win the pot immediately or get value from weaker hands

I'll raise to 700 total (betting 600 more), which applies real pressure while keeping some chips in reserve. This is strong value play with my hand.

I opened to 300 from the button with A6o, and Claude (BB) has 3-bet to 700. This is a relatively small 3-bet (only 400 more for me to call into a pot that will be 1450). Given the 3-handed dynamic and the fact that I will have position for the rest of the hand, I have the correct pot odds to call and see a flop. A6o is a marginal hand against a 3-betting range, but the price and position make a fold too tight and a 4-bet unnecessary. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

With pocket 8s on a flop of Kd 5d 4s, I have a strong overpair. Despite the King on board and two diamonds (creating potential flush draws for Flash), my hand is currently well-positioned in this heads-up pot.

Key considerations:
1. Pocket 8s is ahead of most of Flash's likely range (which includes broadway cards, medium pairs, and draws)
2. The flush draw is a legitimate threat - I need to protect my hand and charge draws
3. Flash has a short stack (6600), so a meaningful bet applies real pressure
4. Checking here would be passive and allow Flash to realize equity with draws or catch up with two more cards

I'm betting 800, which is roughly 55% of the pot. This:
- Protects my hand against flush draws and other draws
- Gets value from weaker hands in his range
- Applies pressure on his short stack that forces meaningful decisions
- Keeps the pot size proportional if he calls and we see additional streets

This is strong value betting with my premium hand in position and against a vulnerable short stack.

Claude 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading for 800 into a pot of 1450 on a K-5-4 board. My hand, Ace-high, has very little equity against a range that includes strong Kings, pocket pairs, and diamond draws. While Claude has shown some aggression in previous hands (like Hand #4), I don't have enough of a piece of this board to justify calling. I have no backdoor draws other than a very weak gutshot to a wheel, and the King is a much better card for the 3-bettor's range than mine. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed pot with Td 9h offsuit. This hand plays well in position and I want to seize initiative rather than just limp. The pot is small (150) and calling 100 gives me position but little fold equity, so I will open to apply pressure and isolate. I will raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a size that balances fold equity and pot control against Claude (SB) and the shorter-stacked Flash (BB). Postflop plan: c-bet when I hit or when the board is favorable for a wide range, otherwise use position to probe and take the pot; be willing to fold to large, committed 3-bets but call small-to-medium 3-bets to realize equity in position.

Folding 2-8 offsuit from the Small Blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker and I'm out of position. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and with poor stack-to-pot ratio and a weak hand, there's no value in calling. My chips are better preserved for premium positions where I have stronger hands. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7 of clubs and 5 of spades. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. I have to call 200 more to see a flop. Although the pot odds are roughly 2:1, 75o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially considering Minni's large stack and aggressive history. Folding is the most prudent long-term strategy here to preserve my stack.

8s3c is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although I'm in position on the button, this hand lacks the equity or playability needed to justify a call or raise. Even with positional advantage, 8-3 offsuit has very poor win probability against the blinds and will require hitting the flop perfectly to continue. Folding preserves chips for stronger opportunities. Flash is short-stacked (6,450), but I should build my stack with stronger hands rather than marginal situations.

I am in the Small Blind with T6o. The action folded to me. While I have the option to complete the small blind for 50 more or raise to steal the blinds, T6o is a relatively weak hand against Minni, who has been active. Since I am out of position for the rest of the hand and the pot is small, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 5-3 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with very little post-flop playability or raw equity. While I have the positional advantage, there is no strategic reason to open-raise with trash hands when the blinds (Minni and Claude) have shown significant aggression in previous pots. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Raise to 300. T9o is a playable, connected hand heads-up — good equity and flop playability. With the button folded it's just me vs Claude in the BB, and a 3x open (300 total) takes the initiative, builds a pot when I have postflop playability, and buys fold equity versus his wide defending range. A min-raise to 200 is too small and allows cheaper flops; 300 is still a tiny fraction of my 20,625 stack so I keep flexibility. If Claude 3-bets big I will re-evaluate (likely fold); if he calls I can continue aggressively on favorable flops.

K2o is one of the worst starting hands in poker - it has minimal equity against Minni's raising range and poor playability postflop. I'm out of position (BB vs SB aggressor), and Minni has demonstrated consistent aggression throughout the tournament. With a weak hand, unfavorable position, and needing to call 200 into a 400 pot with nearly 25 BB still to come, this is a straightforward fold. I should preserve my stack for premium hands where I have a genuine advantage.

K♦2♦ on the button in a 3‑handed game is weak but playable — suited king plus position gives good stealing and postflop potential. I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the blinds, so I’ll open-raise. A standard size here is ~3x the BB (300 total) which gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with these effective stacks. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold K2s; I may consider calling a small 3‑bet from the shorter-stacked BB to see a flop. Postflop plan: use position to c-bet favourable boards (Kx, diamond draws) and proceed cautiously when dominated.

Folding 7-8 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak, marginal hand with limited potential. Minni has demonstrated aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament, and calling from small blind with such a poor hand puts me in a difficult position out of position post-flop. With a reasonable stack (12,725), I should preserve chips by folding unprofitable spots. The 250 to call is not worth committing chips to a hand that will struggle to win against an aggressive raiser, especially when I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10-7 of spades, a decent suited semi-connector. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) folded. To call, I only need to put in 200 more chips into a pot that will total 650, giving me 3.25:1 odds. In a 3-handed game, 10-7 suited is well within the range of hands to defend the big blind with, especially given the attractive pot odds. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the speculative value of the hand justifies a call rather than a fold or a 3-bet.

The flop comes paired with Jacks (Jh 5h Js). Holding 10-high with no direct draw, my hand has very little equity. As the Big Blind defending against a Button raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to see how Minni proceeds; if she bets, I will likely fold given the lack of connectivity and the strength of a Button's range on this board.

I opened K♦2♦ preflop to seize initiative, but the flop J♥5♥J♠ is very unfavourable for my holding. I have no pair, no heart, only a weak backdoor diamond draw, and this paired jack board is heavily covered by the BB’s preflop calling range (lots of Jx and pocket pairs). My fold equity is low and a c-bet will often be called or raised into a hand that already beats me, so I should control the pot and see a turn. Plan: check and reevaluate — call a small probe to realize equity if offered, fold to significant aggression, and look to barreL only on a safe/beneficial turn (K or a diamond) where I pick up real equity or additional fold equity.

The turn card is the Ace of hearts, which is a very strong card for the pre-flop aggressor's range. It also completes a possible heart flush. Since I have no pair, no draw, and zero showdown value with 10-high, there is no reason to bet here. I will check and, if Minni bets, I will fold as the board has become very unfavorable for my hand.

Check. The turn A♥ makes this board very hostile for K♦2♦: I have no pair, no heart, and with the flop/turn showing zero diamonds a diamond-based flush is impossible now — my only realistic outs are three kings and three deuces, which are weak and often still lose to Jx, Ax or any made heart. Both players checked to me on flop and turn, but betting here to try and steal is low EV against ranges that include jacks, aces and heart flushes; I don’t want to inflate the pot with air. I’ll check and fold to meaningful aggression. If my opponent checks back to the river I’ll re-evaluate and only bluff or bet for value on very favorable runouts (or a river king/2 that actually improves me).

The board has run out Jack-high with a pair of Jacks and an Ace. My 10-high has zero showdown value. However, Minni raised from the button and then checked both the flop and the turn. This sequence strongly suggests she does not have a Jack, an Ace, or a heart flush. If she had any of those, she likely would have bet for value or to protect her hand. Since I cannot win at showdown, I will bet 400 into the 650 pot to represent a Jack that was slow-playing or a rivered pair, putting pressure on her likely K-high or Q-high holdings.

Fold. On this paired/hearts-heavy river I have K♦2♦ with no pair and no hearts; there are no more cards to come so I have essentially no outs. Flash’s line (check/call preflop, check-check through turn, then a small 400 raise on the river) is consistent with thin value hands (Jx, A‑x, 5x) and some bluffs, but I’m behind far more often than not. Calling 400 into a 1050 pot (~27.6% pot odds) isn’t justified given my hand’s showdown weakness, and a river raise would be a pure bluff with low expected fold equity here. Preserve stack and fold.

AK is one of poker's premium starting hands, and I'm in an excellent position on the button. I have a significant positional advantage over both blinds, particularly against Minni who is out of position post-flop. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive play that allows me to build the pot, take the initiative, and extract value. This raise is consistent with the action patterns I've observed in recent hands and applies appropriate pressure to the blinds. My stack is healthy enough to play confidently, and Flash's shorter stack (6,750) makes him vulnerable to positional pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. While Claude has been active and aggressive lately, Q9o is a weak hand to play out of position. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot puts me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand without the lead. Given my current stack size and the strength of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve chips for a better spot.

I’m in the big blind facing a button open to 300 with J♦3♠. This is a marginal, unsuited hand and I’ll be out of position postflop, so a 3-bet as a bluff is unattractive — it bloats the pot with poor playability. However Claude has opened to 300 frequently, so his range is likely wide and my pot odds to call are good (calling 200 into a 450 pot; ~31% break-even). With deep stacks I can realize equity and see flops where I can either hit or fold to big aggression. Plan: defend by calling to keep the pot manageable and evaluate on the flop; fold to strong continuation bets if I miss, play aggressively if I connect.

I hold J♦3♠ on J♣ 3♥ 7♥ — that's two pair (Jacks and Threes). Claude opened wide from the button, so his flop range is often single pairs, weak jacks, and some floats/draws. This is a clear value-spot: lead out to extract calls from Jx, 7x, medium pairs and charge backdoor heart/straight draws while still keeping the pot manageable out of position. I’ll bet ~60% pot (400 into 650). If Claude check-raises big I’ll assign him sets/trips and proceed cautiously (fold to extreme overbets, call smaller raises and re-evaluate on the turn).

Raising to 1000 with AK on this Jc 3h 7h board. I have two overcards with strong equity against Minni's likely range (medium pairs, weaker Jacks, broadway hands). My pre-flop aggression and button position give me significant leverage. This raise applies pressure while I'm likely ahead or close to even, builds the pot when I want to, and maintains aggressive play with a premium hand. The sizing is strong but not over-committing relative to my 12,425 stack.

I hold J♦3♠ on J♣–3♥–7♥: top two pair. I led for 400 and Claude check‑raised to 1,000. That sizing is substantial but not an overbet shove; his check‑raise range will contain sets (77/33/JJ), some strong single pair Jx/7x, and some semi‑bluffs (heart/backdoor combos). Calling 600 into the 2,050 pot requires ~22.6% equity — my two‑pair has well above that versus his likely raising range except vs sets, which are relatively rare. Re‑raising to 1,600 would commit a lot more and tend to fold out the weaker hands and draws that I want to keep in; it also increases my risk of getting stacked by a set. Plan: call (1000 total on the flop) to keep worse Jx/7x/3x and hearts in the pot, then re‑evaluate on the turn — extract value or control the pot depending on Claude’s turn action and the card that comes.

I have J♢3♠ — top two pair on J♣-3♥-7♥-T♥. Claude check‑raised my flop bet, which polarizes his range toward sets (JJ/33/77), strong single‑pair Jx/7x, and some semi‑bluffs with hearts. He checked the turn, which makes a made two‑card heart flush less likely (a made flush on the flop would usually continue for value) and shifts his range more toward single pairs and the occasional set slow‑play. I’m ahead of most of that checking range and I want to both get value from worse Jx/7x and deny equity to any backdoor/one‑heart combos that could turn into something on the river. Pot is 2,650 — I’ll bet a firm, but not committing, size of roughly 60% pot (1,600) to extract value and keep weaker hands and some bluffs in. If Claude responds with a very large raise/overbet I’ll re‑evaluate (sets are the main worry and I’ll fold to polarizing overbets); if he calls or makes a small raise I can navigate the river with position and pot odds.

Calling with AK on the turn. I have 8 clean outs (4 Aces + 4 Kings) plus additional equity against Minni's likely range of semi-bluffs and draws. The pot odds of 2.66:1 justify this call given my outs and the turn card (Th) likely helped Minni's range significantly. Folding premium hands with this equity and pot odds would be passive. A raise commits too much given the uncertainty. Calling preserves chips while maintaining reasonable equity to win or improve on the river.

I have J♦3♠ — top two pair (Jacks & Threes) on J♣–3♥–7♥–T♥–A♣. Claude’s line (button open, check‑raise flop, then check‑call on the turn) is polarized toward very strong hands (sets, strong Jx/7x) plus some bluffs/semi‑bluffs (hearts, missed overcards). The river A changes a few bluff/overcard combos into stronger two‑pair (A3/AJ/A7/AT) and completes straights for KQ, so there are hands that beat me; however the bulk of Claude’s continuing range after his turn call are single pairs and busted draws that I still beat. With only one opponent and a pot of 5,850, this is a clear value spot — I want to charge worse Jx/7x/Tx and thin‑value hands while keeping my bet small enough that they’ll call. I’ll bet ~3,500 (~60% pot). If he folds I scoop a healthy pot; if he calls I’m willing to see a showdown. If he raises large I’ll assume sets/straights and fold rather than bloat the pot further.

Calling 3500 on the river with a pair of aces. I have a strong made hand that's ahead of most of Minni's likely value range. The river ace benefited my hand significantly. Pot odds favor this call - I need only ~27% equity and a pair of aces exceeds that threshold against Minni's probable holdings (missed hands, weaker pairs, or bluffs). This is the final street, so calling to see the showdown with a strong hand is the correct play. Stack-wise, I can afford this (leaving 6,325 chips).

In a 3-handed game, K4o is a marginal hand from the button. While it's often an opening candidate, the current table dynamics suggest caution. Claude (BB) has recently lost a significant pot and may be prone to aggressive or tilting play with his remaining 62BB. Additionally, Claude has shown a high frequency of 3-betting my button opens in previous hands (Hands #18 and #21). Given the weak kicker and the likelihood of facing resistance, folding here preserves my stack and avoids a high-variance situation against a potentially unpredictable opponent.

I’m in the small blind with Q♠5♠. Button folded so it’s heads-up vs Claude (big blind). The hand is suited and has decent postflop playability (backdoor flush, occasional top pair) but isn’t strong enough to limp/passively defend. Standard, exploitative line is to raise for a steal: a 2x BB open to 200 gives good fold equity, keeps the investment small (150 more chips from me) relative to both stacks, and lets me take the initiative. If Claude folds I win the 150 pot; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable Q‑or‑spade/texture flops and pot‑control or check on dry/overcards. If he 3‑bets large (well above a small sizing, e.g. 600+ or shove) I’ll fold this hand; versus a small 3‑bet I may call for the playability. Raising to 200 is the right, low‑risk aggressive play here.

With A4o in the big blind facing a raise to 200 from Minni (small blind), I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to call (100 to win 300). While A4o is marginal, several factors favor calling here: (1) The pot odds are favorable - I need roughly 25% equity to break even, and A4o has approximately 35-40% equity against Minni's likely raising range from small blind. (2) One opponent is preferable to multi-way pots. (3) If I hit an ace on the flop, I can win a significant pot. (4) With my stack at 6225 chips, preserving the option to see a flop with reasonable odds is important in a tournament setting. Although I'll be out of position post-flop, the mathematics supports the call rather than folding the big blind without contest.

I raised preflop with Q♠5♠ but this flop (A♦ 8♥ 2♣) completely misses me — I have only Q-high and a runner-runner backdoor spade. Claude called from the big blind with a wide range that includes lots of Ax and pocket pairs that will often continue vs a c-bet, so barreling here is inefficient and will frequently put me behind. With a dry Ace-high board and deep stacks I prefer pot control: check to see his action, call small probes (because of backdoor/spade and Q-high showdown value) and fold to heavy aggression. Checking preserves stack and keeps better bluffs in his range for later streets.

With top pair (Aces) and a 4 kicker on the 8h Ad 2c flop, I should bet for value. Minni's flop check after a pre-flop raise indicates she's either weak or cautious. A bet of 250 chips (60% of the 400-chip pot) extracts value from weaker aces, small pairs, and drawing hands while maintaining good position. This bet is large enough to build the pot when I'm ahead, but not so aggressive that it shuts down the action entirely.

Flop is A♦ 8♥ 2♣ and I hold Q♠5♠ — I missed (only Q‑high and a runner‑runner spade). Claude defended a wide range from the BB so his c‑bet will include many A‑x, some pocket pairs and occasional bluffs. He bet 250 into ~400 (pot now 650). Calling 250 is cheap relative to my 26.7k stack and preserves pot control while allowing me to realize backdoor spade/runner‑runner outs and Q‑high showdown value. A bluff‑raise here is too thin (I have no pair or strong blockers to A) and would bloat the pot unnecessarily; folding surrenders equity for a small cost. Plan: call the 250 and reassess on the turn — continue if I pick up equity or see weakness, fold to heavy turn aggression if I still completely miss.

I have Q♠5♠ on A♦ 8♥ 2♣ 9♣. I currently have only Q‑high — no pair and, crucially, no realistic flush draw (with two spades in hand and zero spades on the flop, you need three spades on board to make a flush and there’s only one street left). I have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~13%), and many of those pairs will still lose to Ax, 9x or pocket pairs that Claude is likely to have given his flop c‑bet. Claude’s flop c‑bet (250) into the ~400 pot looks like a standard continuation with a lot of Ax and some pairs; he’ll continue often on the turn. With essentially no equity and a large stack advantage, I should control the pot and avoid bloating it with junk. Plan: check and see his action — fold to substantial aggression, possibly call a very small probe but not lead out myself. Action: check.

I have top pair (Aces) with a 4 kicker on a board of 8h Ad 2c 9c. The turn brought a 9c, which is a blank that doesn't improve most of Minni's likely holdings.

Key considerations:
1. Minni has checked twice (flop and turn), indicating a weak hand or a draw that hasn't hit
2. I'm likely ahead against her most probable holdings (weaker aces, small pairs, missed draws)
3. With a favorable pot-to-stack ratio (850 chip pot vs my 5875 stack), I should extract value while building the pot
4. The turn 9 is unlikely to have helped her significantly

I'll bet 400 chips, which represents roughly a half-pot bet. This sizing accomplishes multiple goals:
- Extracts value from weaker aces (AJ, AT, AQ)
- Continues to build a pot I'm likely winning
- Avoids overcommitting in case she has a hidden strength (set or hidden draw)
- Maintains fold equity on the river if she doesn't call

Given her passive line, she's more likely to call a moderate bet than fold, which is what I want when ahead.

I have Q♠5♠ on A♦ 8♥ 2♣ 9♣ — I currently have only Q‑high, no realistic draw (with zero spades on board and one card to come I cannot make a flush). I have 6 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 5s) ≈ 13% to pair on the river. The pot is 1300 and it costs 400 to call, so I need roughly 400/(1300+400) ≈ 23.5% equity to break even. My equity (~13%) is well below that. Claude’s line (called preflop, bet 250 on the flop, now 400 on the turn) is consistent with Ax or medium pocket pairs that will often continue and still beat my pair if I hit. A raise to 800 as a bluff is unlikely to fold enough Aces and will often get called or turned into a bigger commitment; it’s unnecessary risk with a big stack advantage. Given the math and ranges, folding and preserving my stack is the best play.

Three-handed on the button with pocket fives (5h5s) and very deep effective stacks. This is a clear open-raise spot: 55 plays well for set-mining and for extracting value/information in position. Calling 100 would invite multiway action and reduce our fold equity; a tiny min-raise is too weak and invites more callers. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300) to isolate and build the pot with position. Plan: proceed postflop based on texture (look to set-mine or play straightforwardly; fold to a large 3-bet if ranges look very strong).

7-2 is statistically the worst hand in poker. Minni raised from the button to 300, and I'm in the small blind facing a call of 250 more chips. While I have a medium stack (67 big blinds), there's no equity advantage here. My hand has almost no playability post-flop, and I'd be out of position against Minni, who has a large chip lead and has shown consistent aggression throughout these hands. This is a straightforward fold - preserving chips for stronger holdings is crucial in a tournament setting.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Minni (the button) has opened for 3x (300 chips). While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a pot of 650), K2o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but K2o often suffers from poor equity realization and kicker issues when it does pair up. Given the tournament context and my current stack size relative to Minni, I will choose to fold and wait for a more favorable spot rather than bleed chips in a marginal situation.

Calling with K2o from the Button in a 3-way pot. While K2o is a weak hand in absolute terms, I have several factors working in my favor: (1) Position advantage as Button - I'll act last post-flop, (2) Pot odds are favorable - calling 100 to win ~250-300 chips, (3) Stack sizing is healthy at 6,725, allowing me to play speculative hands with positional edge, (4) Both opponents have comparable or deeper stacks, so post-flop play will be important. My previous session shows disciplined play - I can exploit position here post-flop. If the flop misses and both opponents show strength, I can fold. If it checks through or I hit a King, I have good value potential. This is a profitable call from Button position with reasonable odds.

QJh is a strong hand in 3-handed play. Since the button (Claude) only limped, I want to take control of the pot and build value or force a fold. Limping behind is an option, but raising isolates the button and puts pressure on the big stack in the big blind. I'll raise to 400 to define the hand strength early.

I have A♥2♦ in the big blind facing a raise to 400 from the small blind after the button limped. This ace is worth defending but has a very weak kicker, so I prefer to see a flop cheaply rather than take an isolated 3‑bet line out of position. Calling (300 more, 400 total committed) gives good pot odds to continue and keeps the pot controllable with my dominated Ax hands. A minimum 3‑bet to 700 is an option to isolate, but with the button still to act and both opponents capable of 4‑betting or calling with better aces, I’d rather call and evaluate the flop — if I hit an ace I can extract value, if I miss I can fold to heavy action or bluff selectively.

Despite being on the Button with positional advantage, K2o is simply too weak to justify calling an additional 300 chips in this scenario. Analysis: (1) Equity problem: K2o has roughly 25-30% equity against two opponents, which is marginal at best for the 300:900 pot odds I'm facing, (2) Aggressive action by Flash and Minni suggests stronger holdings - Flash's raise from SB and Minni's call indicates they likely have better hands, (3) Stack management: My healthy stack of 6,625 means I don't need to gamble on weak holdings early in the tournament, (4) Better spots ahead: I'm only 29 hands in with 121 hands remaining. There will be plenty of better opportunities to accumulate chips. (5) The 100 I already invested is a sunk cost and shouldn't influence this forward-looking decision. Folding here is disciplined poker and preserves my stack for more favorable situations.

I have top pair with a Queen and a Jack kicker on a relatively dry but draw-heavy board (two diamonds). After raising pre-flop and getting one caller (Minni), I need to continuation bet for value and to protect my hand against potential diamond draws or straight draws like 6-7 or 9-10. Minni has a very large stack and has shown a willingness to call or apply pressure, so I want to define my hand early. A bet of 500 into a 900 pot is a standard size to charge draws while getting value from middle pairs or weaker Queens.

Flash is the preflop raiser and has made a small continuation bet (500 into a 1400 pot). I have A♥2♦ — an ace with a very weak kicker and only a remote backdoor diamond possibility. I’m already 400 committed from the preflop call, so calling 500 is cheap and gives me ~26% pot odds to see a turn. I’m in position, so calling preserves flexibility: I can fold to strong turn aggression, or continue/raise if I pair my ace or pick up meaningful equity. A min-raise to 1000 would commit too much with a marginal hand and expose me to 3-bets or tough decisions, so the optimal line is to call and reevaluate on the turn.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a scare card that potentially hits Minni's calling range from the big blind (hands like Ax of diamonds or suited Aces). My hand, while strong on the flop, has now been downgraded to second pair. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot and gauge Minni's reaction. If she checks behind, I likely still have the best hand; if she bets, I will have to evaluate the size and her tendencies before deciding to call or fold.

I hold A♥2♦ on 8♦-5♦-Q♠-A♠ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop; I called to keep the pot controllable and use position. His check on the ace-turn shows either weakness (missed draws, Qx, medium pairs) or pot control/slowplay with a strong hand. Checking back hands a free river to broadway cards and diamond-runouts; I prefer to apply modest pressure to extract value from worse queens/pocket pairs and to deny equity to draws while avoiding over-committing with a poor kicker. I will bet a small probe of 700 (≈1/3–1/2 pot). If he produces a large check‑raise or shove I will treat that as strong and fold; if he calls I will re-evaluate on the river.

I have second pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board that just saw an Ace land on the turn. I checked to control the pot size and because the Ace is a significant scare card that hits Minni's calling range from the Big Blind. Minni bet 700 into a 1900 pot, which is about 37% of the pot. This sizing is relatively small and gives me pot odds of roughly 4:1 to call. While the Ace is dangerous, Minni's aggressive history suggests she could be betting with a draw (like diamonds or a straight draw) or a weaker pair to test my check. Folding here would be too exploitable given the price I'm getting, but raising is out of the question as it would likely only be called by hands that beat me. I will call to see a river card and evaluate.

The river card is the 7h, which doesn't change the board much. Minni has checked to me after I called her turn bet. My pair of Queens has significant showdown value, but it's unlikely I'll get called by a worse hand if I bet, and I'm definitely not folding out an Ace or better. Minni's check suggests she might have been semi-bluffing the Ace turn or has a medium-strength hand like a weaker Queen or a missed draw. Checking back to realize my equity and go to showdown is the safest and most profitable play here.

Board: 8♦-5♦-Q♠-A♠-7♥. I have A♥2♦ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Flash (preflop raiser) c‑bet the flop, checked the turn and then called my small turn probe of 700. That line looks like medium strength (Qx, middle/low pocket pair, some missed draws) rather than a huge value hand; a stronger Ax (AK/AQ) or a set is possible but less likely to have checked and then just called the turn bet. Pot is 3,300 and both stacks are deep relative to this pot. I want to extract value from worse made hands (Qx, 88/55/other pairs, and stubborn callers) while keeping the size small enough to avoid bloating the pot vs a possible better Ace or to induce a big check‑raise. A small value bet of roughly one‑third of the pot (~1,200) is the best line: it gets called by worse hands while allowing me to fold to strong aggression. If he check‑raises large I will respect that and fold; if he calls, I expect to win most showdowns.

On this board of 8d-5d-Qs-As-7h, I have second pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker. I started as the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, but the Ace on the turn is a significant scare card that hits Minni's calling range. After I checked the turn, Minni bet 700 and I called to see if she was testing my weakness or if she really had the Ace. On the river, the 7h is a brick that doesn't complete the primary diamond draw. Minni has bet 1200 into a 3300 pot, giving me pot odds of approximately 3.75:1 (calling 1200 to win 4500). Given Minni's aggressive history and large stack, she could be value betting a weak Ace, but she could also be bluffing with missed diamond draws or middle pairs. Folding second pair for this price in a 3-handed game is too tight, especially against an opponent who applies constant pressure. I will call to realize my equity.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with a suited King (K4d). This is a standard opening hand from this position. I will raise to 3x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

I’m in the small blind with K♥T♣ facing a 3bb open to 300 from the button. KTo is too strong to fold here and I don’t want to simply call and play multiway out of position. Flash on the button has opened wide frequently, and my stack (29,475) is deep enough to apply pressure. I’ll 3-bet to ~3x (900) to get fold equity and try to isolate the button or take the pot preflop. This size is large enough to discourage marginal calls from the big blind but small enough to leave flexibility postflop. If I get called I’ll proceed cautiously OOP, c-bet when appropriate and pot-control with marginal pairs.

I'm facing a re-raise from Minni (Small Blind) who 3-bet to 900 after Flash's button open to 300. I'm in the Big Blind with 5c-Th, a weak hand that doesn't connect well.

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: 5-10 is one of the weakest hands in poker - it has no high-card value and the straight potential is weak (would need a very specific board like 6-7-8-9-J)
2. **Stack pressure**: I have 6525 chips with 800 to call. While I'm getting 2.6:1 pot odds, my hand equity is likely around 30-35% at best against Minni's 3-bet range
3. **Positional disadvantage**: In the big blind, I'll be out of position post-flop if I call
4. **Minni's range**: The 3-bet suggests either a premium hand or an aggressive re-steal given her chip-leader status
5. **Tournament considerations**: With 120 hands remaining, there will be better spots to defend my stack

The risk-to-reward doesn't make sense here. Calling 800 more with such a weak hand when I could fold and preserve chips for premium situations is poor equity.

**Action: FOLD**

I am on the button with King-Four suited and faced with a 3-bet from Minni in the small blind. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, K4s is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially with a stack of only 35 big blinds. Preserving chips for premium positions where I have better hand strength and/or positional advantage is the superior long-term strategy.

Calling 425 on the flop with a pair of eights (98o, hit the 8s on board Kc8s3s).

Analysis:
1. **Hand strength**: I have a pair of eights with a 9 kicker - a solid but vulnerable holding on a flop with a king. This beats underpairs and many unpaired hands but loses to KK, strong Kings, and may be behind to premium pairs.

2. **Pot odds**: Current pot 1275, to call 425 = 3:1 odds. I need ~25% equity to profitably call. My pair of 8s easily exceeds this threshold against Minni's wide BB raising range.

3. **Opponent's bet sizing**: Minni bet 425 into 1275 (roughly 1/3 pot). This modest sizing doesn't scream premium strength like KK or KQ. It appears to be a probing bet or value from a moderate holding.

4. **Minni's tendencies**: From the hand history, he raises frequently and wide from the BB but sometimes checks down hands postflop. He's capable of aggressive plays but isn't polarized to pure premium holdings.

5. **Position and stack depth**: I'm still on the button with last action. My stack is 5925, so 425 represents only 7.2% - a very manageable investment. Position remains valuable for future streets.

6. **Equity assessment**: Against his likely range (medium pairs, Broadway cards, various King combos), my pair of 8s should be ahead more often than not. Even when behind, I have outs.

**Verdict**: The combination of favorable pot odds, positional advantage, modest bet sizing from opponent, and reasonable hand strength makes this a straightforward call. I can easily fold to larger aggression on subsequent streets if the board becomes threatening.

Check. Out of position with 6♥8♦ on 3♠ K♥ 2♠ — no pair, no spade and no realistic draw. Checking controls the pot, lets me call a small continuation bet to see a turn (or fold to a big bet), and preserves chips to apply pressure later or take a free card if he shows weakness. Plan: check-call small c-bets to reevaluate on the turn, fold to large bets unless I improve.

On the flop with Ad Kc against a 3s Kh 2s board, I have top pair with the best possible kicker. This is a strong holding that warrants a continuation bet for several reasons: (1) I successfully took the lead pre-flop with AK, and the flop is relatively dry without obvious draws, making a follow-up bet consistent with my range; (2) Minni's check on a non-threatening board likely indicates weakness or a medium-strength hand rather than a strong holding; (3) With my significant chip disadvantage (8600 vs 31000), I need to be aggressive with strong hands to accumulate chips; (4) A 2/3 pot bet of 250 builds value from weaker holdings while maintaining proper sizing relative to my stack. This bet sets up a manageable pot size on the turn while applying pressure.

I’m OOP with 6♥8♦ on 3♠ K♥ 2♠. I checked the flop to control the pot and Claude c-bet a relatively small 250 into a 650 pot. Calling 250 gives me odds of ~21.7% (250 to win ~1150). I have 6 clean outs to make at least a pair by river (three 6s + three 8s) — roughly a 24% chance to pair by the river — so this is marginally +EV to continue. Against a wide button c-betting range I can also float and potentially take the pot away on the turn or extract value if I improve. I won’t bloat the pot with a bluff-raise OOP with a weak hand, and I’ll fold to a large turn shove/raise unless I improve or get a clear read. Call to see the turn and reevaluate.

I'm out of position with 6♥8♦ on 3♠ K♥ 2♠ 5♥. I have no pair and no meaningful draw — only three 6s, three 8s and four 4s (10 outs) to improve on the river, roughly 10/46 ≈ 21.7% equity. Claude c-bet a small flop bet and then checked the turn; that line often contains Kx, medium pockets and some bluffs. Betting OOP with a weak hand here has limited fold equity against that range and will frequently be called by Kx/broadways, so risking chips to try to take it down now is unnecessary. With a large stack advantage I prefer to control the pot and see a river card for free, folding to any significant bet on the river unless I improve. Plan: check and reevaluate on the river (call small bets with my ~22% equity, fold to large pressure).

Turn bet with top pair/top kicker on a blank. Minni's check indicates weakness, and I need to extract value from this premium holding given my chip deficit. A 500-chip bet is appropriately sized to build the pot and gain information while maintaining stack control.

Fold. On 3♠ K♥ 2♠ 5♥ with 6♥8♦ I have no pair and only 10 outs (3 sixes + 3 eights + 4 fours) ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve by the river. Claude's turn bet of 500 makes the pot 1,400, so a call requires roughly 500/1,400 ≈ 35.7% equity — well above my ~21.7%. His line (button open, flop c-bet, turn barrel) is consistent with many Kx/medium pairs and value hands that already beat me, and raising OOP with this hand has poor fold equity and would bloat the pot against a shorter-stacked opponent. Calling is -EV and risking a raise/shove is unnecessary — fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♦J♦ — a strong, playable hand with both high card value and suitedness. I have position post-flop and a very deep stack relative to the BB (I’m 307 BB; opponent ~91 BB), so I want to apply pressure and steal the blind oftentimes while keeping post-flop maneuverability. A standard 3xBB open to 300 gives good fold equity versus the big blind, isolates weaker holdings, and keeps the pot a manageable size if he calls. If he 3-bets heavily I can fold or re-evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I can play for value/control on most flops. Raise to 300.

Calling 200 to defend my big blind in heads-up with 7h-Th. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), and I have positional advantage post-flop. This is a standard big blind defense in 2-player poker against Minni's frequent aggressive raises. The hand has decent equity and flush draw potential with both hearts.

Checking on the flop with a 4-card flush draw (9 outs) is standard. The paired board is relatively dry, and checking allows me to control pot size while gathering information about Minni's aggression. With decent equity from the flush draw and overcards, I have flexibility to play the turn and river after seeing Minni's action. This is a solid poker decision that respects pot odds and positional advantage.

Heads-up on a dry, paired flop (2h 3d 3h). I hold A♦J♦: no pair but two overcards and a very weak backdoor diamond (needs turn+river diamonds). Claude defended wide preflop and checked the flop, so his range contains a lot of missed hands (Kx, Qx, Jx, weak suited connectors) plus some small pairs and the occasional trap. With position, I want to take the initiative and fold out the unpaired portion of his range while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a standard continuation bet ~1/2 pot (300). This size gets value/folds from many worse hands, keeps most turns manageable, and preserves fold equity. If he calls I can continue with position on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit and fold since I’m only Ace-high.

With 7h-Th on a 2h-3d-3h board, I have a strong 4-card flush draw (9 outs) plus two overcards (T and 7). My hand has approximately 45-50% equity, which is significantly better than the 25% needed to profitably call at 3:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win 900 + my call).

Key factors supporting the call:
1. **Strong drawing equity**: Nine clean flush outs provide substantial winning chances
2. **Favorable pot odds**: 3:1 odds with 45-50% equity is mathematically sound
3. **Heads-up dynamics**: In 2-player poker, drawing hands should be played more aggressively, and Minni's aggression may include semi-bluffs
4. **Position consideration**: While I'm out of position, my flush draw provides flexibility on turn/river actions
5. **Stack depth**: At 8,950 chips with only 300 more to call, I maintain reasonable flexibility

The flop raise from Minni after I checked is consistent with her aggressive play style. This is a +EV call with a premium draw.

Checking the turn with a premium 4-card flush draw (9 outs) plus overcards. Minni checked behind after her flop aggression, indicating a likely marginal holding. Taking the free river card is mathematically optimal with strong drawing equity, preserving my 8,650 chip stack while maintaining reasonable pot odds (25% to 30% hit rate on river with 9 outs). Position advantage post-flop allows me to act last if action continues, and I'll have better decision-making information on river with a made hand or confirmed draw miss.

The turn Qs doesn't improve my A♦J♦ — I still only have Ace-high with a very unlikely runner-runner diamond flush. Claude called my flop c-bet on a paired 2-3-3 board, so his range is heavy with small-to-medium pairs (2x, pocket pairs, occasional 3x/trips) plus some K/Q/Jx and missed draws. Betting the turn as a bluff will get called too often by these hands and only builds a pot I'm usually behind in; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize equity and see a free river. With my deep stack I can still apply pressure later if a favorable river comes or if he shows weakness. I’ll check back and re-evaluate on the river (call reasonable bets for pot odds, fold to large value bets unless I improve or pick up a clear bluff-catching card).

River action with 7h-Th on 2h-3d-3h-Qs-Qd board. My flush draw missed and I hold only a weak pair of Queens with Th-7 kickers. Minni checked back the turn after her flop aggression, indicating a likely marginal hand. Betting a weak pair risks getting called by better hands or raised, while checking minimizes losses and allows me to reach showdown or fold to aggression. This is standard poker - don't bet marginal hands out of position when you have no clear value or bluff. Preserve chips and get to showdown.

Board is Q Q 3 3 2 and I hold A♦J♦ — no pair, just Ace-high. Claude called my flop c-bet on 2-3-3 and then both of us checked the turn; that line is consistent with him having a small/medium pair or even a slow-played Q/3/2 combo that now makes a full house. Any Q, any 3, or pocket 2s in his range now crush me (full house/trips), and I don’t hold blockers to those combos. Betting as a bluff would only fold the absolute air (Kx, Jx) but will be called by most of his pair/full-house holdings; I have poor fold equity and no realistic way to get value. With a deep stack and a relatively small pot, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is to check and not inflate a pot where I have virtually no showdown value. I’ll check to the river and concede this one rather than risk chips on a low-odds bluff.

In heads-up play from the button, I'm getting 3:1 odds to call 50 with 5-9 offsuit. While this hand is weak in isolation, heads-up equity is much wider, I have positional advantage post-flop, and the odds are mathematically favorable. I can fold to aggression if needed or leverage my button position to win the pot. This is a standard call.

Heads-up, button (Claude) has simply completed/limped to 50 — a very wide, weak range. Q♢8♣ is marginal but perfectly playable heads-up and benefits from taking the initiative. Folding 5-9o despite marginal pot odds. While the mathematical case is close (35-40% equity vs 33% needed), the hand is simply too weak to invest significant chips with. With a stack disadvantage against an aggressive opponent, I should preserve my chips for premium spots and better opportunities. This is solid fundamental poker rather than getting drawn into marginal decisions that bleed equity over time.

Heads-up on the button with Qh8s — this is a reasonable opening hand. I prefer to take the initiative rather than limp/call the BB. With TJ on the big blind in a heads-up situation against Minni's 300 raise, calling is the optimal play. T-J is a decent hand in heads-up poker - above average for the position. Checking TJ on an A-9-2 flop is the optimal play. My hand is weak on this texture and doesn't warrant betting into an aggressive opponent who likely connected well with their preflop raise. Calling with T-J on A-9-2. I have 8 outs to a straight (any K or Q), giving me solid equity. Pot odds of 4:1 are excellent for a draw. With T-J high on a 9-2-A-5 board, I have no made hand and no reliable straight draws (can't complete 9-T-J-Q-K or 8-9-T-J-Q with a single river card). I opened preflop and c-bet the A♣ 9♠ 2♥ flop for 300 and Claude called. Turn 5♣ is a blank for my Q♥8♠ — I have Queen-high with no club or meaningful backdoor. River check with T-J high. Minni's flop aggression followed by turn/river checks suggest she likely has a hand that would call a river bet. I have Q♥8♠ on A♣9♠2♥5♣7♠ — Queen high with no pair, no backdoor flush/straight. I c-bet the dry A92 flop and Claude called, then both of us checked turn and he checked river. With 3-6 offsuit, this is a weak holding, but I'm in heads-up play on the Button (Small Blind), which dramatically changes hand selection. I can check down or apply controlled pressure depending on the flop texture and Minni's action. This is a profitable marginal call in heads-up contexts, not a fold.

I’m in the big blind with T♥8♣ (marginal, unsuited) and Claude (button/small blind) just limp-called. Out of position (BB) with T♥8♣ on a 7♣ 2♠ J♠ flop. I have no pair, only ~6 outs to pair by the river and just a backdoor club possibility — very weak equity. With 3-6 offsuit on a 7-2-J flop, I have zero hand value and no draw. While Minni's check suggests weakness, betting here with complete air is just burning chips. With 3-6 offsuit, I have zero hand strength (no pair, no draw) on a 7-2-J-4 board. Minni's two consecutive checks suggest she likely has a real hand. Out of position with T♥8♣ on 7♣-2♠-J♠-4♦-6♥ — I have no pair and no meaningful showdown equity. River check with pair of sixes on 7c-2s-Js-4d-6h. Minni has checked all streets, indicating a weak holding. Heads-up on the button with J♥2♦ — a weak hand but position is powerful. This is a heads-up situation in hand 45. I'm in the Big Blind with K4o, facing a button raise from Minni to 200.

4. Hand strength: K4o isn't strong, but it has showdown value with a King. Against a typical button raising range, I'm getting sufficient pot odds to justify a call.
5. Stack-to-pot ratio: Both stacks are healthy, so this is a reasonable spot to see a flop and play post-flop.

This is a standard call. I should see a flop and continue based on the run-out and opponent action.

K4o on a three-heart flop is a weak holding with minimal equity against Minni's likely calling range. The board is highly coordinated with multiple flush draw possibilities. I opened very wide from the button pre‑flop and Claude just called — his range is wide but he showed weakness by checking this heart‑heavy flop. This is a critical decision in heads-up play. I hold K4o on a three-heart flop (3h 7h 4h), facing a 300 chip raise from Minni after she raised pre-flop.

Key Analysis:
1. Hand Strength: K4 is very weak - I have a pair of fours with a king kicker. On a highly coordinated three-heart board, this is a marginal holding at best.

2. Pot Odds: I need to call 300 into a 700 pot, giving me approximately 2.3:1 odds (need ~30% equity to call). While mathematically tempting, my actual equity is likely insufficient.

3. Board Dynamics: The 3h-7h-4h flop is extremely dangerous with three hearts. Minni's flop raise suggests she either has a strong flush draw, a made hand (pair, two pair, set), or broadway cards. My K4 is likely behind against her most probable holdings.

4. Position & Aggression: Minni is being aggressive in position. In heads-up play, I need to exercise discipline and avoid marginal situations out of position with weak holdings.

5. Stack Preservation: With 7850 chips and the tournament at hand 45/150, folding here preserves my stack for stronger spots where I have better equity and positioning.

K4 simply doesn't warrant continuing in this aggressive pot against an opponent showing strength. This is a fold.

4-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Despite being on the button (best position), this hand lacks sufficient equity to justify a call. Heads-up on the button with K♥3♠. In heads-up play the opening range from the button is very wide and K3o is a perfectly reasonable open. Heads-up on the button with 8c Th I want to seize initiative and open a wide, aggressive range. Checking the dry Ac-5c-3h board with J9o. I have a gutshot to the straight but no made hand. Minni's check suggests weakness or a medium-strength hand. I raised preflop to seize initiative, but this flop (A♣ 5♣ 3♥) is an Ace-high texture that heavily favors my opponent’s calling range from the big blind (lots of A‑x, suited aces, and pairs). Checking the turn with J9 gutshot on a paired board. My hand has limited equity (4 outs to the King). Board A♣-5♣-3♥-3♦ and I hold T♥8♣ — I have no pair and no realistic draw with only one card to come (flush/straight impossible on the river from here). River analysis on a 5c-Ac-3h-3d-3c board with Js9h:

My hand strength: Ace-high with the board's trips (my best holding is A-3-3-3-5). This is a marginal hand on a paired/tripped board.

Action pattern: Both Minni and I checked flop and turn, indicating neither of us has a strong holding. Minni's river check strongly suggests weakness or a marginal hand.

River play considerations:
- A river bet here would be unbalanced as a pure bluff given our passive lines throughout. Minni's check suggests she either has a weak Ace, weak unpaired hand, or is giving up.

River analysis on 6s8s3cJhJs with K9o:

Minni checked on the river, giving up aggression after aggressive play preflop through turn. This is a critical moment.

K9o is King-high showdown value only - no flush draw (only one spade), no straight possibility, just a marginal hand in a competitive range against Minni's wide aggressive range.

**Optimal river play:**
- Checking is the standard decision here for K9o
- We own some portion of Minni's range (weak Ax, missed draws, air bluffs)
- But we lose to sets (66/88/JJ), two-pair combos, and strong holdings like AK/AQ
- Our equity is approximately 25-35% at best against Minni's range

**Why not bet:**
- Betting marginal hands into an aggressive opponent who just checked is typically -EV
- Minni would fold hands we beat but also could trap with strong holdings
- We've already invested 1,600 chips; bluffing here risks further exposure
- In heads-up, checking marginal hands that have showdown value is standard

**Why check:**
- Take the showdown with a hand that has modest but real equity
- Avoid punting additional chips with a marginal holding
- Gather information about Minni's actual hand strength
- Preserve chips for future spots where we have better equity

Checking maximizes expected value with this marginal showdown hand.

4. **Hand Strength**: While J7 isn't premium, in heads-up, it's above the midpoint of reasonable starting hands. It has decent playability with overcards and several draw possibilities.

5. **Tournament Context**: I'm 56 hands into a 150-hand tournament. Playing marginal hands in position with good odds, where I can execute effectively post-flop, is profitable in the long run.

I’m OOP with Qh4h on Jh Kd 7d 6h. Claude opened pre, c‑bet the flop and now barrels the turn for 500 into a 1,100 pot (pot = 1,600 after his bet). Calling 500 to win 1,600 requires ~31.25% equity. I have ~9 hearts + 3 non‑heart Queens = 12 outs ≈ 12/46 ≈ 26.1% to improve on the river, and several of those Queen outs still lose to Kx/Jx which are well represented by his line. A raise would be a thin semi‑bluff and unnecessarily bloats the pot OOP against a sticky/aggressive button. Given the sizing, my raw equity is below the pot‑odds threshold and this is a marginal spot to chase one card — I’ll preserve the deep stack and fold to this turn bet.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) — wide opening range and positional advantage postflop. 2h8h suited is a weak but playable heads-up hand: it can win the blinds outright and has backdoor flush/straight potential. I don’t want to just limp and give Claude a free or cheap flop to exploit me, nor do I want to build a big pot with a marginal hand. Take the initiative with a standard min-raise to 200 (total) to apply pressure, keep the pot manageable, and preserve fold equity. If Claude three-bets big I can fold; if he calls I’ll play position and proceed cautiously on the flop.

Ac5d is a marginal hand at best - an ace with a poor 5 kicker. While I'm in heads-up play and Minni's raise to 200 from the button could represent a wide range, I'm severely out of position as the Big Blind and already short-stacked (10,150 vs 29,550). A5o plays poorly out of position and lacks the strength to warrant getting involved. I need to preserve my chip stack for premium hands where I have better equity. Folding here is the disciplined play to manage my bankroll effectively.

Raising to 250 with T9o on the button in heads-up play. This is a standard 2.5x raise that puts pressure on Minni's big blind. With a shorter stack (10.1k vs 29.75k), I need to play aggressively to build my chip stack. T9o has reasonable playability and is well-positioned to raise here. The raise either wins the blinds or gets called where I have decent equity post-flop.

Heads-up vs Claude's button open to 250 (≈2.5bb). I have 9♦6♠ — a marginal but defendable hand in the big blind against a standard open. Pot is 350 and I only need to put in 150 more (total commit 250), which is cheap and keeps the pot manageable out of position. 3-betting OOP with 6-9 offsuit is suboptimal (poor equity, weak blockers, risks building a big pot without position), so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: continue aggressively with pairs/draws, fold to heavy action on unconnective boards.

Check. I'm out of position with 9♦6♠ on 3♥ J♥ 5♣ — no pair, no real draw and very little equity. Claude opened preflop wide, but this is a relatively dry flop so I prefer pot control and to induce bluffs rather than barreling OOP. Plan: check and evaluate his action — call small continuation bets (≈≤1/3 pot) to see a cheap turn, but fold to any sizable bet or raise (≈≥1/2–full pot). I won't bluff-raise this spot with such a weak hand.

Minni has checked to me on the flop with T9o. While I raised preflop aggressively from the button, my hand on this 3h Jh 5c board is quite weak - I have only a gutshot straight draw (4 Kings) with limited immediate value. The Jack on the board is concerning. Given my significantly shorter stack (9900 vs 29600), I should be cautious about committing chips with marginal holdings. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free, keeping my option open on the next street while preserving chips. If I hit a King for the straight or the Ten improves further, I'll have a stronger hand to play. This is more disciplined than betting weakly with a draw, especially when out of position and short-stacked.

I picked up a pair of nines on the turn (9♦6♠ on 3h Jh 5c 9h). Heads‑up vs Claude’s wide button open, he checked the flop which suggests a lot of non‑made hands in his range; the turn gives me clear showdown value against A/K/Q/x and smaller pairs. I’ll bet for value and protection — a half‑pot size (≈250 into the 500 pot) is big enough to charge heart combinations and get calls from worse hands, but small enough to keep weaker hands and draws in versus a deeper opponent when I’m out of position. If he raises substantially I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large raise given my weak kicker); if he calls I’ll plan to control the river and extract or settle for showdown depending on the card.

Calling Minni's 250 raise on the turn. I hit a pair of 9s on the turn, which is a solid holding in heads-up play. The 3:1 pot odds (750:250) are favorable for my pair. Minni checked the flop passively, and his turn raise could be from a draw, top pair, or pressure. My pair of 9s is likely ahead of most of his range. With 9650 remaining after this call, I maintain chip flexibility for river decisions. This is a standard call with a made hand and good odds.

River: I have a single pair (9s) with a 6 kicker on 3h Jh 5c 9h 4c. Pot is 1000 and Claude (button) called my half‑pot turn bet. His line (open on button → check flop → call turn) contains lots of Jx, some pocket pairs/9x, and heart combos that make a flush on the turn. Many of the hands that will call a turn bet beat my pair (Jx, flushes, two‑pair/sets), while the hands I can extract value from (worse pairs or A/K high) are a smaller slice. Out of position with a vulnerable kicker and a board that gave possible flushes, I prefer to avoid building the pot and getting called by hands that dominate me. Checking to showdown preserves my stack and lets me realize value vs worse while folding out fewer of the better hands. If Claude fires a small bet I’ll call a reasonable size, but I’m not going to turn this into a big pot OOP with such a marginal holding.

I have T9s which made a pair of 9s on the turn. The board is 3h Jh 5c 9h 4c. Minni checked the river, which indicates weakness - likely a missed draw or hand that gave up. My pair of 9s is a reasonable holding that beats many of his possible hands (missed draws, lower pairs). Given his action pattern (raised turn, then checked river), he appears to have had equity on the turn that didn't materialize on the river. A 400 chip bet is approximately 40% of the current pot, which is a standard value bet that should get called by comparable hands (lower pairs, weak top pair) while also being acceptable if he folds. With my short stack (9650), I need to extract value from made hands rather than give free showdowns. This bet puts pressure on him while building the pot.

Claude's river bet is very small (400 into a 1400 pot ≈29% of the pot). I have one pair (nines) with a weak kicker — vulnerable but with real showdown value vs a wide button open. His line (open on button → check flop → call my turn bet) includes Jx, some medium pairs, occasional two‑heart flush combos (needs two hearts in hand) and plenty of missed hands/bluffs. Calling 400 into an effective pot of 1800 requires only ≈22% equity to break even. Given the tiny sizing and the likelihood he has many bluffs/marginal hands that I beat, calling is the +EV play. Raising would be thin and likely get me in trouble vs hands that already beat me; folding concedes too much equity for a small price. I call to reach showdown.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening extremely wide. 6♠2♠ is a weak hand but it's suited and playable in position — raising takes the initiative, creates fold equity, and lets me play postflop with the advantage of position. A minimum raise to 200 (2x BB) is standard here: it pressures the big blind while keeping the pot manageable given the deep stacks. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, otherwise proceed postflop looking to c-bet favorable boards and realize equity in position.

I hold Ah7s in the Big Blind facing a 200 raise (2x BB) from Minni on the button. This is a heads-up situation where Ah7s is a solid holding. The pot odds are favorable at 3:1 (calling 100 to win 300), and Minni's raise from the button is likely a wider range given his aggressive tendencies shown throughout the tournament. By calling, I retain position postflop and can leverage my hand strength, which has good potential against a wide raising range. My stack of 10,950 is sufficient to call and play the hand naturally. A fold would be too passive against this light raise, and a reraise could overcommit me given my chip disadvantage. Calling allows me to realize my hand's equity while maintaining flexibility postflop.

With Ah7s on a Th-Ac-6d flop, I hold top pair against Minni's wide button-raising range. The flop is relatively dry and favors my hand. My action should be to bet and take control of the hand. This applies pressure, extracts value from weaker holdings, and sets up future streets. Sizing around 250 (roughly 60% of pot) is balanced—not too large to overcommit from the BB, but substantial enough to build the pot and discourage speculation. This is a value-focused play that aligns with my strong but vulnerable holding.

I have 6♠2♠ on T♥ A♣ 6♦ — bottom pair. Claude’s 250 into this pot is a relatively small bet in heads-up; it can be value with A/T but also a high frequency c-bet/bluff. With position and a decent amount of showdown equity (I have 5 clear outs: two 6s and three 2s → ~20% to improve by river) and the bet offering ~22% break-even pot odds, the correct line is to call. Calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in, controls pot size, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy aggression on obvious A/T bricks, or extract value/bluff if he shows weakness). I will not inflate the pot by raising here with a vulnerable bottom pair against a potentially strong range.

With Ah7s on Th-Ac-6d-8s, I hold top pair with a reasonable kicker. The turn 8 is a blank that doesn't improve Minni's range significantly. After raising the flop and getting called, continuing to apply pressure with a value bet is the right approach. A bet of 500 (55% of the 900 pot) extracts value from weaker holdings while maintaining stack flexibility. Checking would be passive and allow Minni to steal the pot or control the turn. This bet size is aggressive enough to show strength but not reckless given my 10,600 stack in a heads-up match.

Bottom pair (6♠) on a fairly dry T♥ A♣ 6♦ 8♠ board in heads-up is a hand I want to see the river with, not inflate. Claude’s turn raise to 500 into a 1400 pot is a small-to-medium sizing that can represent thin value (A, T, two pair) but also a wide c-bet/raise frequency. Calling 500 means I must win a final pot of ~2400, so break-even equity ≈ 21%. My clean river outs (two remaining 6s + three 2s = 5 outs) are only ~10.9% to improve, so I’m not calling purely as a draw — I also have current showdown equity vs missed overcards and many of his thin hands. In position I can control the pot, keep bluffs in, and make the correct river decision (value bet if I improve to two pair/trips; fold to heavy river aggression if I miss and he shows strength; or call smaller bets vs thinner bluffs). Raising now would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and gain little fold equity against Ax/Tx. Action: call 500 to see the river and re-evaluate based on the card and Claude’s river action.

River value bet with top pair (Ah7s on Th Ac 6d 8s Qd). Minni checked, indicating weakness or showdown value. My aggressive flop and turn play establish credibility for this bet. The pot is 1,900 and a 1,000 bet (roughly 50% pot) extracts value from weaker Ace holdings while maintaining stack management. The Queen on the river doesn't significantly change equity distribution—I beat all worse Aces and pairs below my top pair. This bet balances my range and maximizes value from hands like AT, A9, A8, A6, TT, 88, 66, and various broadway combinations that can't beat top pair but will call.

Pre-river I planned to call and re-evaluate, but the river Qd changes the math and the likely ranges. Claude has bet every street and now fires 1,000 into a 2,900 pot. Calling 1,000 to win 3,900 requires ~25.6% equity. I currently have a marginal one-pair (6s) with no clean outs on the river — my hand mainly beats pure bluffs and small pocket pairs. Claude’s line (call preflop, bet flop, bet turn, bet river) and the Q improving many of his continuing combos (Qx, Ax, Tx, two-pair/trips) make his range value-heavy here. Raising would only bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and gain little fold equity. My estimated showdown equity vs his realistic range is well below the ~25.6% needed. Therefore the +EV play is to fold and preserve a large stack for better spots.

Heads-up play on the button with Q8o. With only 50 more to call and positional advantage post-flop, I can see a flop cheaply. The hand has playability if I hit, and I can fold to aggression. My short stack (11,950) suggests chip preservation while remaining active is the right balance here.

Heads-up vs a limp from the SB, I want to seize the initiative and isolate. 4c5c is a playable, suited connector heads-up with good postflop playability, so I’ll raise rather than just check. I’ll make it a standard 3x-ish raise to 300 (I’ve already got 100 in; this is +200), which gives me fold equity, keeps the pot manageable (pot is 200 now; if called it will be ~600), and lets me play a position advantage on future streets against Claude’s wide limp-calling range. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll be prepared to fold this marginal hand; if called I’ll c-bet or float depending on the flop texture and pursue equity / initiative.

Calling with Q8o in heads-up play. I have positional advantage post-flop and reasonable equity against Minni's wide raising range. With a short stack, I need to be somewhat active while maintaining chip preservation. The price (200 more to compete for 400) is acceptable for a hand that can flop playable situations. I can use position to control the pot post-flop and fold to aggression if I miss.

Flop is A♥ 9♣ 2♦ and I hold 4♣5♣ — no pair, only a backdoor club draw. Claude limped and called my preflop raise, so his range is wide but contains a lot of A‑x and small pairs that will call a c‑bet. Plan: if he checks back I get a free turn to barrel if I improve or to take a stab on a favourable card; if he bets a small size (≈≤1/3 pot ≈200) I’ll call and float to try to take it away later; if he fires a larger bet (>≈1/2 pot) I will fold and preserve my deep stack. Checking is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line with this particular holding and board texture.

Short-stacked heads-up play where Minni checked a dry flop. Q8d has no made hand, but position and opponent weakness suggest aggression. The A-9-2 board likely missed both of us. Betting applies pressure and allows me to take down the pot now or control the hand. At 11,700 chips, I need to stay aggressive in good spots. This is one - my opponent showed weakness with a check.

I raised preflop with 4♣5♣ and checked the A♥9♣2♦ flop to control the pot. Claude’s bet 250 into the ~600 pot (~41% of the pot) is a relatively small probe and represents a wide range (A‑x, small pairs and lots of missed hands).
