Plan: call to float and see the turn — fold to a large turn raise/bet that denies my equity, continue/pressure if he shows weakness or if I pick up a club/pair/strong draw.

Check. The 7♦ on the turn is a blank for my 4♣5♣ — the backdoor club flush is now impossible and I only have six clean river outs (three 4s and three 5s, ~13%), so my equity versus Claude’s likely calling/betting range (lots of A‑x and small pairs) is very low. Claude’s small flop stab is consistent with a wide range that will call thin; OOP bluffing or barreling here would be thin and frequently called, so betting would be high-variance and +EV only rarely. I’ll check to control the pot and either see a free/cheap river or fold to a sizable turn/river bet. If Claude sizes small on a turn bet I may call to float to the river, but I’ll fold to a larger commitment (>~1/2 pot).

Minni has shown weakness with back-to-back checks on the flop and turn. My Q♦8♦ is a strong semi-bluff with 9 diamond outs to make the nuts flush. Betting ~650 (57% of current pot) applies pressure on a weak opponent, likely takes down the pot immediately given her passive action, and maintains my aggressive short-stack positioning. If called, I retain 10,800 chips with 35%+ equity against most holdings that would justify her call. This is a high-ROI spot in a heads-up high-roller context.

Fold. Claude's turn bet is 650 into a 1750 pot — calling would require roughly 27% equity (650/(1750+650) ≈ 27%). With 4♣5♣ on A♥9♣2♦7♦ I have only six clean outs (three 4s and three 5s) — about 12–13% equity to hit the river, well below the price. Many of those outs still make second‑best pairs versus Claude's likely A‑x/9x/7x value-heavy range given his line (small flop stab then a larger turn bet). A bluff raise here is high-variance and unlikely to be profitable consistently. Preserve chips, fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Jc9d — a playable, aggressive hand in position. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and deny the BB good pot odds to see a flop. A standard 3x-sized open (to 300 total) is consistent with my table image and is small relative to both stacks (I’m ~274bb, Claude ~125bb), leaving plenty of postflop maneuvering room. If Claude folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I have position and a hand with reasonable playability; if he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or flat depending on size/read). Heads-up play from BB requires defending with a wider range. Q9o has decent equity against Minni's button raise and I'm getting 3:1 pot odds. Rather than folding too much, I call to see the flop and play post-flop poker where my positional disadvantage can be mitigated by superior play. The hand has potential to improve and reasonable showdown value.

Checking top pair with a weak kicker out of position is the prudent play. This hand is vulnerable to overcards and higher kickers in Minni's button range. By checking, I control the pot size, gain information from Minni's action, and avoid committing additional chips with a marginal holding. The check allows me to keep my options open on the turn while maintaining positional discipline.

I was the preflop aggressor (opened to 300) and Claude just checked the flop (Th Qc 4c). I hold Jc9d: no pair but a decent semi-bluff — I have 8 outs to a broadway straight (any K or any 8) and a backdoor club flush possibility (Jc blocks some club combos). Heads‑up the checking range is wide (many turned/Q/T miss hands and club draws), so a small continuation bet should pick up the pot often and also gives me fold equity to realize my equity when called. I’ll c‑bet small (~1/3 pot) to pressure unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable vs a shorter-stacked opponent. If Claude folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue aggressively when I improve or have a good read; if he check‑raises big I’ll fold my air.

I hold Q♦ 9♥ on a T♥ Q♣ 4♣ flop, giving me top pair with a 9 kicker. Minni's min-raise to 200 into an 800-chip pot is an unusual bet size that typically indicates either weakness/bluff or a marginal holding trying to keep the pot manageable. I'm getting 5:1 pot odds (risking 200 to win 1000), which is favorable given my hand strength. Against Minni's button/SB range in heads-up play, my top pair has decent equity—I'm ahead of many of their holdings (weaker queens, flush draws, straight draws, air) and have reasonable showdown value. The flop is relatively dry with limited coordinated cards, reducing the likelihood Minni has a strong made hand like a set. Calling allows me to see the turn while controlling the pot size, and if I improve (overcards, my pair strengthens), I'll have opportunities to extract more value. My positional disadvantage (OOP) suggests I should avoid getting into bloated pots, which this call respects. Turn play will depend on the runout and Minni's subsequent action.

I hold top pair (Q♦) with a 9 kicker on a T♥ Q♣ 4♣ J♥ board. Minni's turn check after a flop min-raise suggests weakness or marginal strength. Against their button/SB range, my top pair has reasonable equity and solid showdown value. I'm getting favorable odds to build the pot with a hand that's ahead of many holdings (weaker pairs, draws, air). A 500-chip bet (50% of pot) is appropriate value extraction in heads-up play while maintaining positional discipline and not over-committing if I'm behind. This bet also sets up efficient river play based on Minni's response.

Call 500. I’ve turned a pair of jacks (J♣9♦) on T Q♣ 4♣ J♥ — a medium-strength hand in position. Claude’s small turn bet (500 into a 1500 pot ≈1/3 pot) is consistent with a wide, thin range: value hands (Qx/Tx), missed club floats, and some pure bluffs. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, risk folding out lots of worse hands that will pay me off on the river, and can get me in trouble vs Q/T combos or a reraise. Calling gives correct pot odds (~4:1), keeps his bluffs and worse pairs in, and lets me use position to re-evaluate on the river. Plan: if the river checks I can take a small value stab; if he fires a small/medium bet I’ll often call; if he jams big and I don’t improve I’ll fold (since many of his big shoves are Q/T/two‑pair combos).

Minni's river check after a min-raise flop and turn call pattern indicates weakness—likely a marginal hand or missed draw. My Q♦ 9♥ makes top pair, which has decent showdown value and likely leads against their range. A 600-chip value bet (~30% of pot) extracts value from weaker pairs, air hands, and missed draws while keeping the bet size reasonable enough to invoke calls from inferior holdings. This takes advantage of the positional advantage the checking action has given me.

I call 600. I have middle pair (J♣) in position on T Q♣ 4♣ J♥ 5♦ — a hand with decent showdown value that beats a lot of Claude’s plausible river bluffs (missed club floats, turned air, ace-high or Kx without the 9) and many single-pair hands. Claude’s line (check on flop, small turn stab, and another small river bet) looks like a thin value/bluff mix rather than a polarizing shove; his sizing is small relative to the pot so he’s not polarizing the river heavily. The pot odds are about 4.3:1 (need ≈19% equity) which my pair easily meets against a bluff-heavy range. Raising would bloat the pot and commit me with a marginal kicker against Qx/TX/straights that already beat me, so I keep the pot controlled and call to realize equity and extract value from worse. If Claude jams over my call I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an over‑commit since many of his big shoves are two‑pair/straights).

I'm on the button with Td Ks (Broadway connector with good kicker) against Minni in heads-up play. This is a strong opening hand from the SB in heads-up poker. Looking at the hand history, Minni has been very aggressive and is building a significant chip lead (25,750 vs my 14,100). I need to be aggressive to rebuild my stack. In heads-up play from the button, Td Ks is definitely a raising hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to show strength and take down the pot or play in position post-flop with a hand that has good potential. I'll raise to 300 chips total.

Heads-up vs a BTN open to 300, I'm in the BB with 5c4c. Against a single raiser this is a playable, suited connector with good implied odds given deep stacks (effective ~13.8k). 3‑betting OOP with 5‑4s is too thin — I prefer to call the extra 200 to see a flop and realize equity. I’ll call to 300 total and then evaluate the flop: continue on club/connected or paired boards, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss.

I have 5c4c on Th-4s-Jh — bottom pair (paired my 4) with a weak 5 kicker. The flop contains two overcards (T and J) and two hearts; there are no clubs on board, so I cannot make a club flush with only two streets to come. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable: call small continuation bets to realise equity and fold to large, committed aggression; if he checks back I’ll take a free turn and re-evaluate.

I'm on the button with Td Ks and flopped top pair with a strong kicker (pair of tens with a King). The board is Th 4s Jh. Minni checked to me after I raised pre-flop, showing weakness. 

In heads-up play, this is an ideal spot to continue my aggression. I'm significantly short-stacked (13,850 vs 25,550) and need to build my stack. Top pair is a strong holding on this flop, and a continuation bet is standard when my opponent checks after I was the aggressor pre-flop.

I'll place a solid continuation bet of 400 chips, which is roughly 2/3 of the current pot. This bet:

- Builds the pot when I have a strong hand
- Maintains my aggressive image necessary to rebuild my stack
- Is appropriately sized given the pot and my chip situation

I have 5c4c and paired the 4 on a Th-4s-Jh flop — a weak pair with a bad kicker and only backdoor flush potential. Claude’s 400 into a 1000 pot is a moderate c-bet from the BTN that likely includes a wide range. My hand has reasonable showdown value and can improve on the turn (4,5 or a club runner-runner), so I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn — folding to heavy turn shove/barrel unless I improve or face a highly suspicious line. I won’t raise OOP with this thin holding.

Check. I hold 5c4c — bottom pair (4) with a weak 5 kicker on Th-4s-Jh-3h. The turn brings a third heart, which significantly increases the chance Claude (preflop opener + flop c-bettor) has a heart-heavy hand or a made flush; my hand is very vulnerable and only has 5 outs to improve to two-pair/trips (~10.9% on the river). Pot control OOP is priority: betting here will get called by better hands and fold out very little, while checking keeps the pot small and lets him bluff or barrel off marginal holdings. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call small/medium bets for pot odds but fold to large overbets or shoves unless I hit. If Claude checks back I get a free river and can reconsider.

Minni checked the turn despite the flush-heavy board, indicating weakness or a missed draw. With top pair and King kicker, I have a strong holding. Given my significant chip deficit (13,450 vs 25,150), I need to maintain aggressive play to rebuild. A 750 chip bet (slightly more than half the current pot) applies adequate pressure while preserving some flexibility. This represents a standard turn continuation and capitalizes on Minni's checked strength indicator.

I have 5c4c — a very weak pair (4s on board) with a poor kicker. Board is Th 4s Jh 3h (three hearts). Claude opened BTN, c-bet the flop and now barrels a small/medium turn (750 into a 2150 pot). Pot math: after his turn bet the pot is 2900; calling 750 would make the final pot 3650, so I need ~750/3650 ≈ 20.5% equity to break even. I have 5 clean outs (two remaining 4s + three 5s) ⇒ ~10.9% to improve to two‑pair/trips on the river, so my raw river-out equity is insufficient alone. However, heads‑up BTN open/c-bet/turn-barrel is a wide line that includes a nontrivial number of bluffs and missed overcards (A/K/Q highs, some combos with one heart, etc.), and Claude’s turn sizing is fairly small — meaning a reasonable bluff frequency. Given stack sizes and pot control concerns, calling is the best play: it keeps the pot manageable, realizes my showdown equity when I’m ahead of bluffs, and preserves the option to fold a big river overbet if I miss. Raising OOP with this thin holding would be too loose and commit too much. Plan: call the 750 to see the river; fold to a large shove/overbet if I don’t improve, call a small/medium river bet when pot odds justify.

I have 5c4c — a weak pair (pair of 4s) with a bad kicker on Th 4s Jh 3h 6h. Claude opened preflop, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn, so his range includes lots of hands that beat me (T/J, hearts, two‑pair/sets, overpairs). The river completed a third heart and didn’t improve my hand. Pot control is paramount OOP: thinly betting here will get called by better hands and fold out very little, while check lets me realize showdown value against bluffs/missed overcards. I’ll check and take it to showdown — if Claude now bets a small amount I can call based on pot odds, but I’ll fold to a large overbet/shove since I’m behind most of his believable lines.

Checking the river with top pair King kicker on a four-heart board. The 6h completed potential flush possibilities and Minni's checking pattern suggests either a missed draw or slow-played strong hand. With my shortstack (12,700), risking additional chips by betting is -EV when my hand is vulnerable to flushes and Minni's checks indicate either weakness (in which case they fold my bet anyway) or strength (in which case I'm beat). Taking this to showdown preserves chips for future hands while still having a reasonable chance to win with top pair.

Heads-up on the button with A♣7♥ is a clear open: good position, an Ace-blocker, and a hand that plays well postflop. Calling gives correct pot odds (~28%) to continue against a wide c‑betting range and preserves fold equity on the turn if he shows heavy aggression. I will fold to a large raise/shove and only start building the pot if I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a very strong draw on the turn.

I hold 6♥4♦ on K♣6♦5♦A♣ — a vulnerable bottom pair out of position. Pot is 900 and Claude checked to me on the turn after c‑betting the flop. My earlier plan was to keep the pot controlled with this marginal pair and fold to heavy aggression; that still applies. My backdoor diamond route is dead on the turn, and I only have ~11 river outs to improve to two‑pair/trips (2 sixes, 3 fours, 3 kings, 3 aces) — roughly 24% — and several of those (A/K) also help the button. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a hand that fares poorly on most rivers and invites value raises from better hands. Checking preserves stack, realizes my equity on the river, and lets me fold to large river aggression unless I improve. Plan: check back the turn and re-evaluate on the river (bet for value if I improve, fold to big pressure otherwise).

Betting 500 on the turn after Minni's check-check line. I have top pair (Kd3d on 6d-Kc-5d-Ac) with a strong 9-card flush draw, giving me excellent equity. Minni's double check suggests weakness—she likely has a marginal hand or is trying to float. This bet applies pressure to extract value from weaker holdings, generates fold equity with my semi-bluff, and maintains aggression in this heads-up match. The pot will be 1400 after my bet, setting up river play with a strong hand.

I have a vulnerable bottom pair (6♥) on K♣-6♦-5♦-A♣ and Claude has shown a fairly value-heavy line (button open, flop c‑bet, turn barrel after the A). Pot is 1,400 and it costs 500 to call. My clean river outs to improve to two‑pair/trips are roughly 11 (2 sixes, 3 fours, 3 kings, 3 aces) → ~11/46 ≈ 24%. Break‑even equity for this call is 500/(1,400+500) ≈ 26.3%, so raw draw equity is already slightly below the pot‑odds threshold. Worse, many of those outs (A/K) are “dirty” — they often improve Claude as well (or give him trips), so my true effective equity is lower. I’m out of position and this hand is marginal; raising would be reckless and calling sets me up for a tough river facing further pressure. With a healthy stack advantage and plenty of hands left in the match, folding and preserving chips is the +EV line here.

Raise to 200. Heads-up on the button I should open a wide range and K♣8♥ (K8o) is a perfectly acceptable raising hand. A 2x BB (200 total) is standard sizing: it maximizes fold equity against the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (I have ~228 BB), and allows me to leverage position postflop if Claude calls. If he 3-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate textures and use position to extract value or control the pot.

Playing 9dJc from the big blind in a heads-up matchup. Minni raised to 200 from the button (50 SB + 200 raise). I need to call 100 more into a 300 chip pot, getting 3:1 odds. 

In heads-up poker, blind defense is critical and I should defend a wide range. 9dJc is above-average for a big blind defense hand - it's a broadway card with decent playability postflop (can hit top pair, make straights, etc.). 

At this stage of the tournament (hand 83 of 150), I have a healthy stack of 16990 and stack sizes are relatively close (Minni 22710). I should see the flop and make decisions based on the board texture and action. Folding too many hands here would be exploitable and cost me EV in the long run.

Calling is the standard play here.

On the flop with 9dJc against 6s Ts 4c. Minni checked, giving me the initiative. My hand has solid playability here - I have a gutshot straight draw (any K or 8 makes a straight), two broadway cards with overcard potential, and reasonable showdown value. In heads-up poker, taking the initiative with decent hands is standard. A flop c-bet around 1/3 pot (150 chips into a 400 chip pot) applies pressure, allows me to build the pot when ahead, and extracts value from Minni's wider defensive range. The board texture isn't particularly dangerous for my hand, making a bet the natural play.

Call 150. Claude’s bet is a small probe into my BTN opening range; I have K8o (no pair) but I’m in position and the bet is cheap. Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity — I only need ~18% to break even on this call and my hand has reasonable showdown/runner equity against a wide continuation-bet range (floating hands, small pairs, draws). Raising would over-polarize my range and commit more with no made hand; folding is too passive. Plan: see the turn in position — if I pair a K or get a favorable card (club/backdoor help or a blank that improves blockers) I can apply pressure, otherwise I’ll check and fold to large aggression.

With Jack-high and only pair outs (6 outs = ~13% equity), betting the turn is not profitable. Minni's check suggests weakness or a hand that doesn't want to build a large pot. I control variance by checking, maintain positional advantage for the river, and allow Minni to define their holding further. Against a checking range, my showdown value is minimal and I should avoid committing additional chips without clear value.

Board is 6♠ T♠ 4♣ 2♣; I hold K♥8♣. Claude led the flop with a small 150 stab then checked the turn — that line looks like weakness or a pot-control/medium pair range. Heads‑up and in position I want to apply pressure with a hand that has showdown value but is also a good bluff candidate. Betting half the pot (≈350) gives me meaningful fold equity (break‑even fold frequency ≈33%) to take down the 700 chip pot against many missed draws and marginal holdings, without overcommitting with no pair. If he folds I win a good-sized pot; if he calls I can often check the river and fold to heavy aggression, and if he check‑raises I’ll release. This balances risk and reward in a deep‑stack heads‑up spot and maximizes my EV over many similar situations.

Calling the check-raise on the turn with J-high. Getting 4:1 odds (need only 20% equity), I have approximately 40-45% equity with 12 overcard outs. In heads-up play at this stack depth, folding J-high to a turn check-raise is exploitable. Minni's line (flop check, turn check-raise) suggests a medium-strength hand or semi-bluff rather than value. The pot is 1400 to call 350, making this mathematically profitable and strategically sound for maintaining heads-up range balance. I'll see the river and reassess.

River check with J-high. Minni's action pattern (turn check-raise, river check) indicates weakness. While J-high has limited showdown value, it's sufficient against a checking range likely containing whiffed draws and marginal hands. Checking allows me to realize showdown cheaply without risking additional chips. Betting would either win a pot I already have access to via showdown or lose to hands that beat J-high. The check-through maximizes value in this situation while minimizing downside risk.

I bet the turn and Claude called, so his range now is biased toward made pairs (T/6/4), small pockets, or spade draws. The 7♠ on the river completes many spade draws — I hold K♥8♣ with no spade and have essentially no value to extract. A bluff here is high-risk: a half‑pot bet (~700) needs ~33% folds to break even, but his line (call/call) makes him likelier to call with completed flushes or medium pairs. I planned to check the river if he called the turn to avoid over‑committing with air, so I will check and go to showdown; if he had instead fired a large bet I would fold to heavy aggression.

Holding A9s on the button in heads-up play is a premium hand that warrants aggression. A9 suited has strong potential with ace-high and a flush draw possibility. In heads-up poker, position is critical and we should be raising a wide range of hands from the button. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is appropriate here - it's large enough to assert dominance and extract value, but not so large that we're overcommitting with a hand that can easily fold to significant pressure. Minni is the bigger stack, but we maintain fold equity and can win the pot immediately or see a flop in position where we can make decisions with better information.

Heads-up vs a 300 open, T9s is a very playable, suited connector with good equity and blocker value. I’m out of position post-flop, so I want to take the initiative rather than just defend and play a tricky OOP pot. A 3-bet to 1000 (~3.3x the open) generates fold equity against Claude’s wide button opening range while keeping the pot controlled with deep stacks. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll c-bet many favorable flops (spade/connected boards) and play for value; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and often fold to extreme aggression.

This is a heads-up confrontation where I raised to 300 from the button with A9 suited, and Minni has re-raised to 1000 (approximately 3x). In heads-up poker, re-raises are more common with a wider range, and A9 suited is a premium hand with solid equity. 

A9s has several advantages:
- Ace-high strength
- Strong flush draw potential (9 of clubs with two clubs in hand)
- Good equity (approximately 45-50%) against Minni's typical re-raising range in this spot
- Favorable pot odds: I'm risking 700 to win 2000, which is 2.86:1

Given the stack sizes and action, calling is superior to folding (too tight) or re-raising (committing more chips with a marginal hand). Calling allows me to reach the flop and make better decisions with more information while realizing my equity. The pot currently stands at 1300, and I can see a flop in position to evaluate my hand strength.

I have T9s on a 4c-3s-Tc flop — top pair with a good 9-kicker after I 3‑bet preflop. Claude’s call of my 3‑bet keeps a wide calling range (broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors, some club combos). I want to take and keep the initiative: this board gives opponents many hands that will call a bet (weaker tens, second pairs, floats) and a few club-based one‑card draws that I need to charge. Betting denies equity, builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, and extracts value from worse hands. Size: ~50% pot (pot = 2000) is large enough to charge draws and get value but small enough to avoid committing too much when out of position. If Claude folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he raises big I’ll fold or proceed cautiously given the possibility of sets (33/44/TT) or strong two‑card club combos.

Calling with A♣9♣ on a 4♣3♠T♣ flop. The nut flush draw with 9 outs, excellent pot odds of 3:1, and position advantage justify the call. My equity is approximately 45-50% against Minni's range, making this a clear value play. Calling allows me to realize my equity, maintain position on later streets, and avoid overcommitting with a drawing hand.

I have top pair (T) with a decent 9-kicker on 4♣-3♠-T♣-2♥. I c-bet the flop (1,000) and Claude called — that call represents a wide, mostly medium/weak calling range (weaker tens, second pairs, broadway floats and some one-card club draws). The turn 2♥ is a largely blank card: it only helps a narrow set-of-2s combo and does not complete club draws (only one board card left, so a flush is impossible for me and requires two more clubs to complete for many draws). I’m out of position, so I should keep the initiative, charge drawing hands and extract value from worse pairs/floats. Pot = 4,000 — I’ll bet ~50% pot (2,000). This size is big enough to deny correct odds to many draws and to get calls from worse tens, 9x and broadway floats, while not overcommitting OOP. If he folds I take it; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river card and his range. If he check-raises large on the turn I’ll assign him a very strong range (sets/full houses) and fold or proceed very cautiously depending on sizing.

Calling with A♣9♣ on a 4♣3♠T♣2♥ board. This is a nut flush draw with 9 outs and ace-high. Receiving 4:1 pot odds (2000 to win 8000) while holding ~35-40% equity makes this a profitable call. Raising would be speculatively aggressive without a made hand, and Minni is unlikely to fold given the action and heads-up dynamics. Calling maximizes my equity realization on the river while maintaining a sustainable stack position.

I have T♠9♠ on 4♣-3♠-T♣-2♥-6♠ — top pair with a good 9‑kicker. I took the initiative (c‑bet flop, bet turn) and Claude passively called both streets, which looks like a wide calling range (weaker tens, second pairs, medium pocket pairs, and some broadway floats). The river 6♠ is a fairly benign card — it only pairs small 6x hands or gives a spade but does not complete straights/flushes that beat me. Checking surrenders value to many worse hands that will call a river bet. I want to extract value while keeping the pot manageable OOP, so I’ll bet for value ~½ pot. Size: 4,000 chips. If Claude reacts with a large check‑raise or shove I’ll treat that as a polarized, very strong range (sets/two‑pair) and fold or re‑evaluate based on sizing.

River decision with A♣9♣ on 4♣3♠T♣2♥6♠. No flush completed - only ace-high remaining. Minni's consistent 4-street betting pattern indicates a made hand (pair or better). To call 4000 into a 12000 pot requires 25% equity, but my ace-high likely has only 20-24% equity against Minni's probable range of pairs, two-pair, or straights. This is a mathematically marginal situation slightly favoring a fold. Sunk cost fallacy cannot justify calling with a -EV river spot. Preserving remaining stack (8390) for future hands provides better expected value than this marginal all-in-adjacent decision.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Q♣3♦ (Qh 3d). Blinds 50/100, pot 150 and it’s only one opponent — opening very wide from the button/SB is standard. I'm in the BB with A♣K♠, one of the premium hands in poker. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button/SB. This is a clear raise situation for several reasons:

1. **Hand strength**: AK is the best non-pair premium hand and plays excellently heads-up
2. **Minni's min-raise is suspicious**: A min-raise from 100 to 200 suggests either weakness or a trap, and either way I want to apply pressure
3. **Position/stack dynamics**: We're heads-up, and while Minni has a stack advantage (27.4k vs 12.3k), AK is strong enough to play for value now
4. **Value extraction**: I want to build the pot with this hand against a likely weaker range

I'll raise to 600 total (adding 400 to the pot), which is a standard 3x raise that builds the pot while keeping it manageable. This applies pressure, gets value, and gives me good pot odds if I win preflop or hit the flop.

Claude 3‑bet to 600 after my min‑open to 200. I hold Qh3d — a weak but playable hand heads‑up with a queen blocker. Calling 400 more (600 total) into an 800 pot gives ~25% break‑even equity and is cheap enough to see a flop with positional advantage. I won’t 4‑bet bluff with Q3o (too often dominated and not worth inflating the pot). Plan: call now, c‑bet thin on dry/Q‑high flops, exercise pot control on coordinated/flushy boards, and fold to large 4‑bets or heavy action unless I improve.

Betting for value with top pair, top kicker (A-K on a K-high board). This is a strong hand that should extract value from Minni's likely weaker range. A 500 chip bet (~42% of pot) is a standard value bet size that keeps Minni in the hand while building the pot. His pre-flop min-raise followed by call to my 3-bet suggests weakness rather than strength, making this an ideal spot to bet and build a pot I'm ahead in.

I have Q♥3♦ on K♦6♠7♠ — no pair and no flush draw. Claude 3‑bet preflop and now fired 500 into a 1,200 pot (pot is 1,700); calling 500 gives me ~22.7% break‑even. Heads‑up Claude’s c‑bet range is wide so there are many missed hands I can pressure later from position, but I don’t have enough equity to raise profitably here. I’ll take a cheap look at the turn with position, float if appropriate, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression unless I pair up or see a clear bluffing line. Call the 500 (total committed 600 preflop + 500 = 1100).

Betting 800 on the turn for value. Minni's check indicates weakness - his min-raise preflop and call to my 3-bet followed by calling the flop bet and now checking the turn all suggest a marginal holding. I have top pair, top kicker, which is likely the best hand. A value bet of approximately 36% of the pot (800 out of 2200) keeps Minni in the hand while building value and maintains fold equity if he's on a complete air bluff or weak hand. This is standard value extraction in a heads-up match where I'm ahead.

Heads‑up vs a 3‑better: Claude’s range is wide but still contains a lot of value. I hold Q♥3♦ on K♦‑6♠‑7♠‑2♦ — no pair, no spade draw. Pot is 3,000 and his turn bet is 800, so I’m getting ~21% pot‑odds to call (800/(3000+800)). I have ~6 clean outs to a pair (3 queens + 3 threes) → ~13% to improve by showdown, so calling purely to hit is slightly -EV. However, in heads‑up play Claude has incentive to barrel thin and bluff sometimes, and his bet sizes on flop/turn have been relatively small. With position I can float now, use river to probe for weakness, or attempt a well‑timed bluff if he shows weakness. Raising here would be a thin, low‑equity bluff against a 3‑bettor and I’d rather keep the pot controllable. Plan: call the 800 to see the river, fold to strong river aggression unless I pair or get a clear river bluffing opportunity.

I have Kings and Sevens with an Ace kicker on a K-6-7-2-7 board. This is a strong two-pair hand. Minni's preflop min-raise followed by calling three streets and checking the river indicates a marginal/weak holding rather than strength. The river blank doesn't significantly improve his range. I should bet 1000 for value extraction - this sized bet allows Minni to call with weaker holdings (weak Kings, lower pairs, busted draws) while giving me proper value from my premium hand. My remaining stack of 9490 after this bet is still healthy, and this maximizes the value I extract from this favorable situation.

Claude 3‑bet preflop and has barreled flop and turn — that line is heavily weighted to value (Kx, sets like 77/66, overpairs) and the river pairing the board increases those combos. The pot is 4800 and his bet is 1000; calling would commit 1000 to a 5800 final pot so I need ~17.2% equity to break even (1000/5800). I hold Q♥3♦ with no pair and essentially zero river outs — I only beat pure air/bluffs. Given his line and range, the frequency of bluffs is likely below the threshold and a raise as a bluff would be too risky against a 3‑bettor who has shown consistent aggression. Fold — preserve the stack and wait for a better, clearer spot to apply pressure.

In this heads-up scenario, I'm on the button (small blind) with J3o. While J3 is ordinarily a weak hand, the dynamics shift significantly in heads-up play:

1. **Position Advantage**: As button, I act last postflop, giving me significant positional leverage
2. **Pot Odds**: I'm getting 3:1 odds (150 to call 50), which is favorable
3. **Range Requirements**: In heads-up, button ranges are much wider than in full games. Folding J3 would be too nitty
4. **Stack Depth**: With ~95bb, I'm deep enough to play speculative hands with positional advantage

Minni has been playing a reasonable range from the big blind, and my J3 will have implied odds and flexibility postflop. The plan is to use position and aggression to win pots, especially if the flop is dry or I can represent strength. Calling here is standard heads-up strategy and allows me to apply maximum pressure with my positional advantage.

I’m in the big blind with 5♣8♥ and Claude (SB) just limped. This is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position post‑flop. I’ve already got 100 in the pot and there’s no bet to call, so the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and see a free flop. Raising to try to steal risks getting called by better hands and playing a big pot OOP with a marginal holding; with both stacks deep I’d rather keep the pot small and only invest further if I hit or pick up a clear fold. Plan: check, evaluate flop (continue only with a pair, strong draw, or a favorable read).

Flop is T♦ Q♠ K♥ and I hold 5♣8♥ — I completely missed. There’s no realistic draw (with one heart in my hand I cannot make a 5-card heart flush by river, and there’s no straight draw), so my hand has essentially zero showdown value. Claude limped preflop (wide/weaker range) but I’m out of position and the pot is small (200). Betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and leaves me OOP if called. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and only invest further if I pair or pick up a real draw or get a clear read/cheap bluffing spot. If Claude leads into me on the flop I’ll fold most of the time unless it’s a tiny probe bet that offers correct pot odds to call.

Checking with J3o on a Td Qs Kh flop. My hand is too weak to bet—I have only jack-high with a four-card gutshot draw (four outs). The board is highly coordinated and favors typical big blind ranges. Minni's flop check could indicate medium-strength holdings. Stack depth (70bb) and weak hand strength argue for taking a passive line here. Checking allows me to see another card cheaply and fold if threatened, or hit the gutshot for a strong hand. Betting would be speculative bluffing with insufficient equity in an unfavorable spot.

Check. I have 5♣8♥ on T♦ Q♠ K♥ 3♦ — no pair and no realistic draw with one card to come, so essentially zero showdown equity. Pot is tiny (200) and I’m out of position vs a limp/caller (wide but often contains high cards). Betting here would be a thin, high-variance bluff OOP with poor fold equity; best line is pot control. I’ll check the turn, fold to any meaningful bet, only consider calling a very small probe that gives correct pot odds to chase a lone pair, and if checked through to the river reassess (thin value bet if I pair, otherwise concede).

Checking bottom pair on the turn. Minni's flop and turn checks indicate weakness or medium strength. My pair of 3s has decent showdown value here. Betting would risk ballooning the pot with a marginal hand. Checking preserves pot control, allows me to see the river cheaply with potentially the best hand, and gives me better information for river decision-making.

I missed the board with 5♣8♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Pot is tiny (200) and Claude limped preflop then checked the flop and turn, so his range is wide but contains many hands that will call a small river bluff. Betting here is a thin, high-variance bluff OOP with poor fold equity; the risk (creating a bigger pot with zero showdown equity) outweighs the small chance of taking it down. Best line is to concede the hand and check to showdown for free. If Claude bets over me I’ll fold most hands unless it’s an extremely small probe that gives correct pot odds to call.

River showdown with pair of 3s. Minni has checked the flop, turn, and river, indicating weakness throughout this hand. My pair of threes with Jack kicker likely has the best hand or is close to it. Betting doesn't gain value from worse hands (they fold) and risks losing to better holdings (which may call). Checking preserves pot control on this scary, coordinated board and allows us to reach showdown—the appropriate conclusion to a hand where both players have shown weakness.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. J♣3♦ is marginal but playable in position and raising takes the initiative. I’ll make a small standard raise to 2x the big blind (200 total) to get fold equity, isolate the BB, and play the flop in position. If Claude 3-bets hard I can fold this weak hand; if he calls I can use position to apply pressure or realize my equity cheaply.

2-4o is the worst starting hand in poker with minimal equity against any range. Despite having 3:1 pot odds to call, the hand is too weak to play from the big blind. Even if Minni is stealing frequently, this particular hand lacks the playability to justify entering the pot. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

Pocket kings is a premium hand and one of the strongest starting hands in poker. In a heads-up situation on the button, this is an excellent opportunity to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Strategy: Raising to 300 (total) accomplishes several goals:

4. Leaves room for both competitive action and fold equity

Recent history shows Minni plays reasonably tight, so aggressive play here with premium cards is warranted. Kings hold up well in heads-up play and benefit from aggressive pre-flop betting.

Heads-up vs Claude (button/SB) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 8♠2♦ — essentially the worst offsuit hand. I’ll call to see a flop and play passively out of position: give up to significant aggression on dry boards, only continue with a pair (8s/2s), two pair or better, or a very strong draw. I completely missed this flop. Board Q♠ K♠ Q♦ vs my 8♠2♦: no pair, only a very remote backdoor flush (needs both turn and river). Flopped a full house (Kings full of Queens) - one of the strongest possible hands in poker. Opponent checked, indicating weakness or a medium hand. Board Q♠ K♠ Q♦ vs my 8♠2♦: I have a spade draw (two spades on board + my 8♠) — 10 spade outs. Required equity to call is 500 / (1100+500) = 31.25%. Call 500.

I’m out of position with 8♠2♦ on Q♠ K♠ Q♦ 3♥. I only have a one-card flush draw (10 spades unseen) and a few small pair outs — roughly 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the flush on the river. Turn action with Kings full of Queens (KK on board QsKsQd3h):

I have flopped an extremely strong hand - Kings full of Queens. The turn brought a 3h, which doesn't improve Minni's likely holdings or provide any concerning new possibilities.

Minni's line (check-call flop, check turn) suggests a medium-strength hand rather than a monster. The most likely holdings are:

- This line is inconsistent with QQ, which would more likely bet or raise for value

With such a premium holding (full house), I need to extract maximum value. A bet of 900 achieves several goals:

This sizing gives Minni proper odds to call with marginal hands but still builds the pot for a strong finish on the river.

I have 8♠2♦ on Q♠ K♠ Q♦ 3♥ — no pair and essentially no realistic outs. Claude’s line (open pre, flop c-bet, now a 900 turn bet) is strongly value‑oriented (lots of Qx/Kx/trips in his range). That is well below the required equity, and even if I pair the river I’ll often still be behind. Raising would be a thin bluff OOP with poor blockers and high risk of getting stacked. I’m on the button (small blind) with pocket Aces — the best heads-up starting hand. There’s 50 to call and min-raise is 200. With deep stacks and an opponent who shows willingness to call and play big pots, I want to raise for value and isolate. A standard 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) builds a healthy pot, keeps many worse hands in, and gives me position postflop to extract more value. I’ll re-evaluate on a 3-bet (call or 4-bet shove depending on size) or continue betting for value on favorable runouts.

Folding Q6o from the Big Blind. While Q6 offsuit provides decent pot odds (2:1), the hand is simply too weak to get involved with. Minni has been aggressive throughout, but this isn't a hand worth defending the blind with. With 61 hands remaining in the tournament and reasonable stack depth, there's no need to get into marginal situations. I should preserve my 14,990 chips and look for premium holdings or better spots to accumulate value. This is a standard fold in this situation.

Heads-up on the button with 4-3o (offsuit). While this is a weak hand in isolation, standard GTO heads-up strategy dictates raising a very wide range from the button to apply pressure and win pots. Even the weakest hands benefit from aggression when in position. At minimum, a raise to 200 chips forces Minni to make a decision - either fold and concede the pot, or call with a wide range where I'll have decent equity post-flop. Given my chip disadvantage (14,940 vs 24,910), I need to be aggressive and accumulate chips. A minimum raise applies maximum pressure while committing the fewest chips if called. If Minni folds, I win the pot immediately; if called, I maintain position with a playable hand.

Claude opened to 200 from the button/SB. Pot is 300 and I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. Th8d is a marginal but perfectly reasonable defense heads-up — against a wide SB open it has decent equity and post‑flop playability. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot small with this hand rather than 3‑bet bluff and bloat the pot out of position. Plan: call 100 (total 200) to see a flop; continue with top pair or strong draws, pot‑control with marginal hands, and fold to clear strength/overwhelming aggression.

Board is Qc Ac 2c — a three‑club flop. I hold Th8d: no pair, no club, and essentially no meaningful equity versus a wide button open that contains lots of A‑x, Q‑x, club draws and pocket pairs. Claude can c‑bet this board very often and will have a lot of hands that beat me or have a flush draw. I want to keep the pot small with a worthless hand: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude checks back I can evaluate the turn for a possible bluff or continue to pot‑control; but I will not build the pot out of position with nothing.

Check back to see the turn card cheaply. With 4-3o on a high board (Q-A-2), I have virtually no equity and minimal showdown value. Taking a free card is prudent when holding a weak hand with poor prospects. If I improve on the turn, I can continue; if not, I can fold without committing additional chips. My short stack status makes chip preservation important in marginal spots like this.

Board is Qc Ac 2c 6d; I hold Th8d — no pair, no club, essentially zero meaningful equity. Both checked the flop, which usually indicates either a missed holding or pot‑control with Ax/Qx/pocket pairs; the turn 6d changes nothing for my hand. Betting OOP here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity (Claude can have A/Q, a club, or a pocket pair and will often call or raise), and with deep stacks I don't want to bloat the pot with nothing. I’ll keep the pot small and check. If Claude fires a sizeable bet on the turn I’ll fold; if he checks the river I can reassess and consider a light bluff only on a very safe brick, otherwise check down.

With 4-3 offsuit on Q-A-2-6, I have complete air and zero equity. However, Minni has checked both the flop and turn, indicating weakness. In heads-up play, my aggression from position combined with her passivity makes this an ideal bluffing opportunity. A 200-chip bet (roughly half-pot) applies pressure while being reasonable relative to my short stack (14,790 chips). I'm trying to win the pot now rather than hope for a miraculous river card. The fold equity here outweighs any potential showdown value from this worthless holding.

I checked the flop and planned to fold to meaningful turn aggression with Th8d — the 6d changes nothing for my hand. Claude checked the flop (range: lots of Ax/Qx, pocket pairs, some clubs) and now fires a 200 probe into a 600 pot. Calling 200 to win ~800 requires ~25% equity. With only T8o I have ~6 clean outs to pair (~13% on the river) and near‑zero equity against hands that already beat me (Ax/Qx/pairs/club made hands). Even though the bet is small, it’s a value/probe against missed hands and calling here is a negative‑EV float that can lead to larger commitment out of position. Preserve stack, avoid turning a marginal situation into a bloated pot — fold.

Heads-up on the button/SB with 9sTs — a very playable, suited connector with good equity and great post‑flop playability. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to seize initiative and build a pot in position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — large enough to avoid giving a free cheap flop and to fold out the weakest BB hands, but small enough to keep many dominated or speculative hands in. With deep stacks (≈248 BB) I can navigate postflop — C‑bet favorable textures, float or barrel when appropriate, and respect/defend vs large, polarized 3‑bets. Raise to 300 to take control and maximize equity in position.

9-2 is the worst starting hand in poker (the "crabs"). While I'm getting 2:1 odds to call (200 to win 400), this hand has minimal equity against Minni's raising range in heads-up play. Minni has been consistently aggressive throughout this match, and I lack the hand strength to profitably contest this pot. Folding preserves my stack for stronger spots where I have better equity and control over the hand. This is a straightforward fold.

Heads-up on the button with As3c. This is a classic button-raising situation in heads-up play. While As3o is not premium, position is crucial in heads-up poker, and I should be raising a wide range from the button.

Key factors:
1. Position advantage: I act last postflop
2. Hand has showdown value (Ace) and can improve
3. Standard raising size: 3x BB = 200 chips
4. Opponent (Minni) has been aggressive but also folds to aggression
5. Stack situation (15k vs 24.8k) doesn't dictate folding marginal hands in position
6. Recent history shows my aggressive play has been winning

This is a standard button raise. I'll raise to 200.

Heads-up in the big blind with 3s4h facing a standard button open to 200. Pot is 300 and I must call 100 more (total contribution becomes 200). Calling here is cheap — I get ~20% pot odds — and Claude is opening a wide button range, so 3s4h has reasonable equity versus that range. I don't want to 3-bet bluff this weak, unsuited hand out of position with deep stacks; that would risk more and be easier to exploit. I’ll call to realize equity and play the flop carefully: check/fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card boards, but continue or apply pressure on low/connected textures or if I pick up improvement.

I have 3s4h on a 6s-3d-Ac flop — a weak made hand (pair of threes) with a poor 4-kicker on an A-high, relatively dry board. Claude opened from the button, so his range contains a lot of A‑x and broadway hands that dominate me. My priority is pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Checking here lets me (a) induce a continuation bet I can call with reasonable odds against a wide BTN range, (b) realize equity cheaply if he checks back, and (c) fold to heavy aggression or a large check-raise which will usually denote an Ace or better. Plan: check; call small–medium c-bets (roughly up to ~1/2 pot) to keep the pot manageable, fold to big aggression, and if I improve (another 3 or a 4 for two pair) or see clear weakness on the turn I'll change gears and apply pressure or value bet.

I have two pair (Aces and threes) on a 6s3dAc flop with As3c. This is a strong hand requiring a value bet. Minni's check indicates weakness. A 280 bet (approximately 2/3 of the 400 pot) is standard sizing to extract value from worse hands and protect against turn cards that could help my opponent. This commits me to continuing if called but maintains reasonable stack control in our heads-up match.

I checked the flop with 3♠4♥ on 6♠-3♦-A♣ to keep the pot small with a weak made hand (pair of threes). Claude’s bet of 280 is a medium-sized continuation bet and gives me ~40% pot odds to call; against a wide BTN range I have enough showdown equity and 5 clean outs (two threes and three fours) to justify a call. I want to realize equity and keep the pot controllable out of position — fold to substantial further aggression (big raise/shove) but re-evaluate on the turn if I improve or see clear weakness. Calling is the low-variance, correct play here.

Heads‑up in the big blind with 3♠4♥ on 6♠-3♦-A♣-8♣. I currently have a vulnerable pair of threes with a weak kicker and only ~5 clean outs (two remaining threes, three fours). Claude (BTN) opened pre and c-bet the flop — his range contains lots of A‑x and broadways that dominate me, plus some bluffs. With deep stacks and the pot already at 960 my priority is pot control and avoiding getting blown off my hand (or committing to a big pot) OOP with a marginal holding. Checking the turn keeps the pot manageable, lets me call small–medium bets to realize equity, and fold to large or polarizing aggression which will usually denote a better hand. If Claude checks back I go to the river with the same plan; if he bets ≤ ~1/2 pot (~480) I will call to chase my outs, but I’ll fold to pot-sized or larger overbets/shoves unless I improve. If I hit trips or two pair on the river I can extract value. For these reasons I’ll check the turn.

Betting 650 on the turn with two pair (As3c on 6s3dAc8c). Minni has shown weakness by checking twice. The 8c is a safe card. This sizing (roughly 2/3 pot) extracts value from worse hands in Minni's range while maintaining reasonable pot control. Strong position and strong hand warrant continued aggression when opponent is weak.

Heads‑up OOP with 3♠4♥ on 6♠-3♦-A♣-8♣. I have a vulnerable one‑pair (pair of threes) and only 5 clean outs (two threes + three fours) ≈ 5/46 ≈ 11% to improve on the river. Claude’s line (BTN open, flop c‑bet, turn bet) is consistent with a lot of A‑x and broadway value hands that dominate me, though he may also have missed draws or occasional bluffs. The turn bet of 650 into a 1610 pot gives me ~29% pot odds to call — not enough for a pure draw, but calling is the low‑variance, correct play to realize showdown equity and keep the pot controlled OOP. Raising as a bluff here is too risky (no good blockers, poor kicker) and will be called by better hands or get 3‑bet. Plan: call the 650 to see the river, fold to a large raise/shove, fold to a pot‑sized overbet on most blank rivers, but extract value if I hit trips/two pair or if I see clear weakness on the river.

Board is 6♠-3♦-A♣-8♣-J♣ and I hold 3♠4♥ — a marginal one-pair (trip blocker none, weak kicker) that loses to any A-x, any Jx, two-pair+, and even any two clubs (opponent having two clubs has a flush). Claude opened BTN, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; his check on the river is a sign of weakness or pot control with a marginal A/x or missed draw. I have no club blockers and very little showdown or bluff equity here — a bluff is thin and likely to be called by hands that beat me or fold only to a sizing that’s too risky to commit with this holding. A thin value bet is also poor because very few worse hands will call. Given I can see showdown for free and avoid turning a small margin into a big mistake, the low-variance, correct play is to check back and show down.

River value bet with two pair (As3c). Minni has checked the river showing weakness throughout the hand. My two pair should win against most of his checking range (weaker aces, pairs, missed draws). Betting 550 chips (about 1/4 of pot) extracts value from worse hands while maintaining reasonable sizing. This amount is optimal for getting called by weaker holdings while not overcommitting to the pot.

Heads‑up, I have 3♠4♥ on 6♠-3♦-A♣-8♣-J♣ — a vulnerable one‑pair (pair of threes) with a weak kicker. Claude opened BTN, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; that line is weighted toward A‑x, Jx, and occasional strong pairs/sets. His tiny river bet (550 into a 2810 pot) is a probing/blocking sizing or a thin value bet — it also allows a fair number of bluffs to remain in his range. Calling 550 to win ~3360 requires ~550/3360 ≈ 16.4% equity. My hand’s absolute equity vs a value‑heavy range is low, but vs a mixed river range (thin value + bluffs) my equity is in the neighborhood of the break‑even point, and the call is cheap relative to my 23,780 stack (≈2.3%). Raising as a bluff is unattractive — I have no good blockers and would face calls from better hands or a shove. Folding would concede the pot cheaply but risk laying down the best hand to a tiny probe. Low‑variance + exploitative play here is to call and see showdown.

Heads-up on the button with Td5d — a suited, playable hand with position and backdoor flush/straight potential. Calling 50 is cheap (pot 150 → 50 to call) but I want to seize initiative and apply pressure with a standard heads-up open. Raising to 3x the big blind (total commit 300) gives good fold equity, is consistent with my raising frequencies here, and keeps the pot size manageable relative to our deep stacks. Plan: if called, continue aggressively on favorable flops and float/check-bet as appropriate; fold to a large 3-bet (re-evaluate only vs a small 3-bet because stacks are deep). Raise to 300 (total in pot) to maximize EV from position while preserving fold/escape options.

In heads-up play from the big blind, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (400 in pot, 200 to call) to defend against Minni's button raise. While K-3o is marginal, it has reasonable showdown value and I'll have position postflop. Minni has been active from the button throughout the tournament, so I should defend my blind with a wider range. The call size (200) is only 1.2% of my 16,670 stack, making this a reasonable spot to see a flop and play my hand based on the board texture and action. Folding too frequently to button raises would allow Minni to exploit me.

I'm in the big blind heads-up against Minni with K♥3♣ on a flop of K♣Q♣8♣. While I have top pair, my kicker is very weak, and this is an extremely dangerous flop for my hand. The three clubs create numerous flush draws and flush possibilities, and my K-high is vulnerable to many of Minni's likely holdings from a button raise.

At this stage of the tournament with a 16,470 chip stack facing a 22,930 chip opponent, I need to play a solid, measured approach. Checking allows me to:

1. Keep the pot small with a marginal hand (top pair, weak kicker)
2. Realize showdown value without overcommitting
3. See another card before putting more chips at risk
4. Respond to Minni's action on the turn with better information
5. Avoid getting trapped in an expensive pot when I'm likely in a dominated position

If Minni checks back, I gain a free card and can reassess on the turn. If she bets, I'll have a better picture of her holding and can make a more informed decision about whether to proceed. Given the vulnerability of K-3 on such a flush-heavy board, discretion is the better part of valor here.

I was the preflop raiser and I have position — standard heads-up strategy is to continuation-bet a wide range. The flop Kc Qc 8c misses my Td5d entirely (no pair, no club) but Claude checked, which gives me fold equity against many of his calling hands (Ax, small pairs, unpaired broadways) and lets me seize the initiative. The board is somewhat wet (club flush possible, K/Q combos), so I size to deny equity but keep future options: bet ~2/3 pot. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I assume Kx/Qx/club draws and will give up to heavy turn aggression or fire a controlled second barrel only on safe non-club blanks; fold to sizeable raises since my hand has almost no showdown value. Bet 400 into the 600 pot to maximize immediate EV while keeping the line manageable on later streets.

Folding K♥3♣ on K♣Q♣8♣ against Minni's 400 raise. While I have top pair, my kicker is critically weak (worst possible king), and this board is extremely dangerous. The three-club board creates numerous flush draw possibilities and flush completions that beat my hand. Minni's raise on the flop indicates strength, and I'm likely facing a dominated position (stronger king, pair, or flush draw). My K-3 is vulnerable to:
- Any king with a better kicker (KQ, KJ, KT, K9, K8)
- Sets (QQ or 88)
- Strong flush draws
- Ace-high hands

With 16,470 chips and facing 22,530, I don't need to defend this marginal hand. The fold saves chips for a better spot where my hand has higher equity. Position and stack dynamics will provide future opportunities with stronger holdings. Discretion is warranted here.

Pocket nines is a strong hand in heads-up play from the button. In this two-player situation, I have a significant positional and hand strength advantage. Looking at the recent hand history, Minni has been aggro-raising pre-flop frequently, but I've been winning consistently with well-timed aggression. 

A raise to 250 total (200 additional chips beyond my 50 SB) is a reasonable 2.5x raise that builds the pot without overcommitting with a strong but not premium hand. This puts me in control of the action and allows me to play the hand with position on all future streets. My stack is healthy enough to play aggressively with a pocket pair, and I expect to win this hand equity pre-flop against Minni's opening range in this spots.

Claude opened to 250 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with 8d5h offsuit — a marginal but defensible hand heads-up. Pot is 350 and calling 150 (bringing my total contribution to 250) gives ~30% pot odds to see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop, keep the pot controlled, fold to strong continued aggression on dry boards, and look for opportunities to bluff or pot-control on favorable textures.

Flop is Td Qh 2s and I hold 8♦5♥ — no pair and no meaningful draws. In heads-up the button’s opening range is wide, but this board is very dry and many hands in his range (any Q, any T, most pocket pairs, broadways) comfortably beat me. I’m out of position for the rest of the hand, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. I’ll check to keep the pot small, see if he checks back, and fold to significant sustained pressure. If he fires a very small bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈150) I may consider a float, but I’ll fold to larger bets (~≥1/2 pot) and look for better spots to bluff or to build a pot when I have equity.

Minni checked the flop, indicating weakness. With pocket nines on a Td-Qh-2s board, I'm in a good spot to apply pressure with my positional advantage. My nines are an underpair to the Broadway cards, but they have reasonable equity and the board composition suggests Minni likely missed or has a marginal holding. This maintains my aggressive image and keeps Minni from getting free cards to improve. I've been winning consistently with positional aggression against Minni throughout this session, and this spot follows that profitable pattern.

I defended preflop with 8♦5♥, but this flop (T♦ Q♥ 2♠) is very dry and I have no pair and effectively no realistic backdoor draws. Claude’s c-bet of 300 into the 800 pot requires ~27% equity to make a call profitable. Out of position with deep stacks I don’t want to float with air and create difficult spots — this is a clear fold. I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot to exploit heads-up.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠8♣ — a playable, positionally strong hand. I want to take the initiative rather than just call 50 and let the BB see a cheap flop, so I’ll open-raise to apply pressure and gain fold equity. Stacks are very deep (~232bb), so I’ll keep the raise a standard size to keep weaker hands in and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Raising to 300 total (≈3×BB) balances these goals: it denies free cards, defines Claude’s range, and lets me play postflop in position. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed cautiously); if he calls I’ll use position to continue aggression when appropriate.

This is a straightforward fold. I hold 2d-8d, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker (often called 'the worst hand'). While the pot odds are decent at roughly 3:1, I have minimal equity against Minni's raising range from the button/small blind. Even in heads-up play where ranges are wider, 2-8 offsuit is a clear fold situation. My hand has virtually no playability postflop and will lose money in the long run if I call. Preserving my stack by folding weak hands like this is essential for tournament success, especially with 55 hands remaining.

T7o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant aggression at this stage. While I have positional advantage as the button/SB, I'm significantly shorter-stacked than Minni (16,570 vs 23,280). With 55 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve my chips and wait for higher-quality hands - premium pairs, strong broadway cards, or better connected hands that play well both preflop and post-flop. Folding weak marginal hands when short-stacked is a fundamental strategy to extend my tournament life and improve my equity.

Heads-up on the button with 3s2s — a weak but playable suited wheel draw. Calling 50 into a 150 pot is cheap (~25% immediate pot odds) and preserves position postflop, which is where this hand earns most of its value (disguised straights/flushes, two-pair/sets rarely). A min-raise to 200 is an option to apply pressure, but with such a marginal hand I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop; I can fold to strong 3-bets or continue aggressively on favorable flops. Plan: call now, evaluate flop and use position to bluff or extract value when appropriate.

Raising to 200 total (100 more chips) with QhTc in heads-up position. QT is a solid HU hand with playability, and I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. This sizing is appropriate—not too aggressive given the hand strength, but assertive enough to build the pot and take control. The moderate stack depths support this aggression while maintaining flexibility post-flop.

Heads-up on the button with 3s2s: a weak but playable suited wheel. Claude’s raise to 200 in HU is likely wide, so calling 100 more (total committed = 150) is cheap — you’re getting roughly 25% immediate pot odds to see a flop (100 into ~400 total). Keeping the pot small and using position postflop preserves options: you can realize equity with disguised straights/flushes, bluff on favorable textures, or fold to significant aggression. If Claude 3-bets heavily I’ll fold most of the time; if we see a favorable flop (spade/low connected) I’ll continue aggressively. With these stacks there’s good implied odds to hit disguised nutted hands, so call and evaluate the flop.

Betting 200 (0.5x pot) with top pair on a relatively dry flop. QhTc has made a pair of queens on Qd7dAh, which is the best hand currently in a likely range of hands Minni holds post-raise. The weak Ten kicker dictates a controlled bet size rather than overcommitting. In position, I should take control before two diamonds complete to a flush. This bet size gets value from weaker queens, flush draws, and mid-pair hands while keeping the pot manageable given my kicker vulnerability.

Call. Pot is 600 and calling 200 gets me ~25% immediate pot odds (pot to win ~800), which is cheap enough to realize equity in position. 3♠2♠ has almost no showdown value on Q♦7♦A♥, but it has backdoor straight/flush potential and excellent implied odds heads‑up — and Claude’s flop raise in HU is often wide. Raising as a bluff here is too risky (I’d get called/3‑bet by lots of better hands); folding relinquishes position and too much dead money. Plan: call to see the turn; continue only with a meaningful improvement (pair, spade, 2/3/4/5 runouts) or take a free showdown if he checks; fold to heavy turn pressure without improvement.

Turn bet with trips (QhTc on Qd7dAhQs board). The second queen gave me three of a kind, an extremely strong holding in this heads-up matchup. Minni has shown weakness through the hand (only calling), suggesting they likely have a marginal hand like AX or a draw. Betting 400 (0.5x pot) extracts value while keeping them in the hand. This sizing balances getting paid with hands like A7/A9 or draws (which I dominate) while not overcommitting with a hand this strong. My positional advantage and trips justify aggressive value extraction on the turn.

Fold. On Q♦ 7♦ A♥ Q♠ with 3♠2♠ I have essentially no equity — only three 2s + three 3s = 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13%) to pair on the river. The pot is 1200 and Claude’s turn bet is 400, so I’m getting ~25% immediate pot odds to call, but my raw chance to improve (and actually beat his likely raising range of Qx/Ax/strong draws) is well below that. A raise here would be a thin bluff that won’t fold out top pairs and needlessly inflates the pot out of position in future hands. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button in a heads-up situation with 5-7 offsuit, one of the weakest starting hands. However, the dynamics favor calling here: (1) I only need to call 50 more to complete my small blind, giving me excellent pot odds for a speculative call; (2) in heads-up play, ranges are much wider and position matters significantly; (3) I'm already committed 50 chips, so the marginal cost is minimal; (4) even weak hands like 5-7 can play profitably postflop with position in a 2-player game against an aggressive opponent like Minni. I'll call and take a flop, where I can leverage my button position and stack depth to make better decisions based on the community cards.

Heads-up Claude limp-called the small blind, which indicates a wide/passive range. Qc6s is a playable hand that fares well vs a limp-call range, so I want to take the initiative without bloating the pot out of position with deep stacks. I make the minimum raise to 200 to isolate and build a pot where I can c-bet on many flops, but keep the size small enough to retain postflop flexibility (check/control on coordinated boards, fold to heavy aggression when I miss).

Folding 5-7 offsuit from the small blind despite favorable pot odds. While the 3:1 odds and button position are appealing in isolation, 5-7o is fundamentally too weak in tournament poker. It has poor postflop playability, minimal equity against most of Minni's range, and doesn't justify playing a marginal hand in a high roller event. At this stage (hand 98/150), maintaining strict hand selection is more valuable than chasing marginal pot odds. This is the standard GTO play from the small blind in heads-up.

Heads-up on the button with A♦Q♥ and deep stacks — this is a clear raising spot. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to merely call; A-Qo is ahead of the BB’s folding/calling range and gains value and initiative by raising. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total) — big enough to fold out weaker hands and get value from worse A/Kx, but small enough to keep pot control and to handle a 3-bet without overcommitting. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and stack depth; calling/passive play would surrender position and EV. Action: raise to 300 total.

6-10 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant defending the big blind against Minni's raise. While Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament, 6-10o lacks the equity needed to profitably defend out of position against a wide opening range. Even at favorable pot odds (200 to call into 400), I need a significantly stronger holding to defend the BB. With a healthy 16,770 chip stack and 51 hands played of 150, I can afford to be selective and fold marginal hands. I'll look for better opportunities to accumulate chips.

Raising K5o from the button in heads-up play is standard strategy. King-high has decent value, and positional advantage is critical. A raise to 300 (2.5x BB) applies reasonable pressure while maintaining good bet sizing. This is well within my raising range as the button, and I expect to either win the pot immediately or play postflop with position. The slightly shorter stack doesn't change the logic here—I should still play positionally aggressive poker in mid-tournament spots.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♠Q♥ facing a 300 open from the button. AQs is a strong, high-equity hand heads-up and is ideal to 3-bet for value and fold equity against what is likely a wide opening range from Claude. I want a size that (a) extracts value from worse Ax/Kx/pair hands, (b) denies equity to speculative hands, and (c) keeps stacks deep enough to play post-flop. Raising to 1000 total (adding 900 more) is a solid size — if called the pot will be ~2000, so I risk 900 to win 2000 (breakeven ~31%), which AQs comfortably meets vs a wide calling range. If Claude 4-bets big I will re-evaluate, but for now 3-betting for value/fold equity is the optimal play.

K5o is a marginal hand that I can profitably raise as the button in heads-up play due to positional advantage. However, when facing a 3-bet from Minni to 1000, the situation changes significantly.

Key considerations:
1. **Hand weakness**: K5o lacks the strength to call a 3-bet profitably. It's essentially a flip at best against Minni's likely 3-betting range (which includes pairs, broadway cards, and strong hands).

2. **Stack commitment**: Calling 700 more commits me to a 15,770 chip stack after the flop with a weak hand. This is problematic for postflop play since I'll often be dominated or forced into difficult decisions.

3. **Gap concept**: While K5o is in my button opening range, it falls outside my calling range against a 3-bet. There's a significant equity gap between opening and facing aggression.

4. **Tournament stage**: At hand 100 of 150, I should be selective about marginal situations where I'm likely behind.

Folding preserves my stack and allows me to enter pots with stronger holdings where I have better equity and decision-making clarity.

Heads-up on the button with K♠6♦ — a hand worth opening. I’ve already posted the SB and the pot is small, so I want to assert aggression and take advantage of position while keeping the pot manageable. A standard 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) pressures the big blind’s wide defense range, preserves postflop maneuverability with a deep stack, and limits my commitment if Claude 3-bets. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, call smaller or single raise if the price is right, and use position to continuation-bet favorable flops or control pot size when I miss.

J7o is a weak hand that doesn't justify defending out of position against Minni's raise. While Minni has been aggressive, J7o lacks the equity and playability needed to call from the big blind. With 50 hands remaining and a solid chip stack, I should fold marginal hands and wait for better opportunities.

Raising with T9 suited to 250 total (100 additional). This is a standard heads-up button raise that takes initiative, builds the pot with a playable hand, and allows me to leverage positional advantage post-flop. T9s has good equity with straight and flush potential. The raise is appropriately sized to control the action while not overcommitting.

Heads-up in the big blind with Q♠9♦ facing a raise to 250 from the button. With stacks deep (≈235BB) and the raise relatively small, calling 150 (total commit 250) gives ~30% pot odds and is the correct defensive play — Q9o has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range and decent postflop playability. Out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with a light 3-bet, and folding is too tight given the odds. Plan: call, see the flop and proceed based on texture (continue with top pair/strong draws, otherwise play cautiously OOP).

Heads-up, out of position on A♦5♦5♣ with Q♠9♦. I completely missed the flop — I have no pair and only a backdoor diamond (9♦). Claude’s small preflop open suggests a wide range that contains many Ax hands and other hands that will c-bet; I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a weak hand that folds to pressure. Check for pot control and information: fold to a sizable c-bet, consider calling a small probe for pot odds, and if checked to take a free turn to improve or bluff later if the texture allows.

Continuation bet on the flop with T9c. My semi-bluff has strong equity through flush draws and overcard outs. I'm in position, Minni checked showing weakness, and a 300 bet (60% pot) maintains aggressive initiative while building the pot with good draw potential. This is standard heads-up play with my hand and position advantage.

Heads-up OOP on A♦ 5♦ 5♣ with Q♠9♦ — I have no pair and only a backdoor (runner-runner) diamond. Claude’s bet of 300 into a pot of 800 is a small c-bet (~37% pot) and is consistent with a wide betting range (Ax, many broadways and missed hands). Q9o vs a wide c-betting range has roughly that ballpark of equity and gives me a chance to realize equity or see a cheap turn. Raising OOP with no pair would bloat the pot and put me in difficult spots — not optimal. Plan: call, keep the pot controllable, fold to heavy turn action if I don’t improve, but play aggressively if I pair or get a favorable runout/check-back.

Check. I’m out of position with Q♠9♦ on A♦5♦5♣3♣ — I have no pair and my backdoor diamond draw is dead after the turn. I only have 6 outs to pair (≈6/46 ≈ 13% on the river), so I can’t profitably call a sizable bet on the turn; purely by pot-odds math I’d only continue to very small probes (≈≤160 into the current ~1100 pot). Checking controls the pot, lets me see a free river, and preserves folding to a large turn bet or calling a tiny probe — if checked to on the river I can consider value/bluff depending on the card (Q/9 or a favorable bluff spot).

Minni has checked twice (flop and turn), indicating weakness. I have T9c with strong semi-bluff equity - a 9-outer flush draw plus additional straight draw outs (any 2 or 4 gives me an open-ended wheel draw, with the 4 being particularly valuable). Current pot is 1,100. A turn bet of 550 (~50% pot) applies pressure to Minni's weak range while building the pot with a hand that has significant equity. This bet either wins the pot immediately or improves my fold equity while maintaining the ability to execute on strong river cards. The sizing is appropriate for my stack depth (15,820) and keeps me in control of the hand.

I’m out of position with Q♠9♦ on A♦-5♦-5♣-3♣ and have no pair; my backdoor diamond is dead. The pot is 1,650 and the turn bet is 550, so a call requires ~25% equity (550 / (1650+550) ≈ 25%). Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♥6♦ — this is a strong heads-up hand because of the Ace blocker and positional advantage. Calling with A4o from the big blind against Minni's button raise. The hand has reasonable equity with the Ace, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, and my post-flop skills should be profitable. With A4o on a paired board (Ts Jd Th), I have ace-high which is marginal at best. I'm out of position and facing a wide raising range from Minni's button raise. Heads-up on a paired 10xJ board I have no pair but I do have the Ace blocker and position. Folding A4o on a paired board (Ts Jd Th) against Minni's continuation bet. In heads-up play from the button with 2h-Th, calling the 50 additional chips is optimal. Heads-up in the big blind with Q♠8♥ — a marginal but playable hand with some showdown value and backdoor possibilities. Heads-up in the big blind with Q♠8♥ on K♠ K♦ 5♣. I have no pair and this is a very bad board for my hand — the button's calling range contains lots of Kx and stronger hands that dominate me. Holding 2h-Th (ten-high with backdoor flush draw) on a board of 5c-Ks-Kd. Opponent Minni checked the flop, indicating potential weakness. Heads-up, out of position on K♠ K♦ 5♣ T♦ with Q♠8♥. I have no pair and no meaningful draw — my hand is dominated by the Kx portion of his check/call range. The turn card (Td) significantly improved my hand from ten-high to a pair of tens with king kicker. Minni's two consecutive checks on the flop and turn indicate weakness in his range. Fold. On K♠ K♦ 5♣ T♦ with Q♠8♥ I have no pair and no real draw. Claude’s turn bet 130 into the ~200 pot (pot now 330) gives me pot odds of ~28.3% to call (130 / (330+130)). Heads-up on the button with A♥Q♠ — a very strong opening hand in this spot. Heads-up with A♥Q♠ vs Claude’s 3-bet to 900 and deep effective stacks (~150bb) — calling is the best line. Betting 1200 on the 5d 6h Js flop with AQd. This is a premium hand with strong equity (13+ outs to a made hand or two-pair, with flush and overcards). Call 1200 (total committed 2100). I have A♥Q♠ — two overcards to a dry J‑6‑5 rainbow board and good equity versus a heads‑up 3‑better. Betting 2000 on the turn with AdQd on 5d 6h Js Ks. This is a premium drawing hand with 18+ outs (9 flush outs, 3 non-diamond 9s for straight, 4 non-diamond Ts for straight, plus pair outs). Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop bet 1,200, turn raise to 2,000) is value‑heavy — likely Kx, Jx, or sets — though some bluffs/semi‑bluffs are possible. River value bet with the nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T). This is the best possible hand on this board. I've been aggressive throughout the hand with AdQd and now have a made hand. River completed Broadway for me: A♥Q♠ makes A–K–Q–J–T (broadway). With the nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T on a 5d-6h-Js-Ks-Ts board), I have the best possible hand. I have A♥Q♠ — the river T♠ completes Broadway (A–K–Q–J–T). The only hands that beat me are spade flushes (villain must hold two spades). My Q♠ is a valuable blocker to many two‑spade combos. 3s Td is a weak hand that should be folded in most positions. While I only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop and the pot odds are favorable (3:1), my actual equity with this hand is minimal. Heads-up on the button with K♥3♣ — marginal but playable in position. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but in heads-up I should open a wide range and seize initiative. Folding 2-9 offsuit from the big blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with poor equity against a button raise. QT of hearts is a strong hand in heads-up play, especially with positional advantage on the button. Minni has shown an aggressive playing style throughout the tournament. Heads-up facing Claude’s raise to 200 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 9♦5♠ — a weak, unsuited hand and out of position postflop.
