Flop: A♣ K♦ 5♥. I hold 9♦5♠ — middle pair with a weak kicker and I’m out of position. Claude opened wide heads‑up, so his range contains a lot of A/K and many hands that beat me. Continuation bet with QhTh on AK5 flop. I have a gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush draw with reasonable semi-bluff equity. Minni's check indicates weakness. I checked the A♣ K♦ 5♥ flop with 9♦5♠ intending to call small continuation bets but fold to heavy pressure. Claude’s bet is 280 into the pre‑bet 400 (pot now 680) — a ~70% pot sized bet. Heads-up on the button with A♥4♦ — a strong, positionally advantaged holding. Q2o is too weak to defend from the big blind out of position, even with 2:1 pot odds. I'll play tighter from the blinds and invest chips in better hands. My stack position allows for selective play.

Raising to 200 from the button in heads-up play. In heads-up, the button should be aggressive to win uncontested pots or play in position. If Minni folds, I pick up 150 chips; if called, I have position and can play the flop carefully.

Heads-up vs the button (small blind) opened to 200 (2x). I have A♠9♦ — a strong heads-up hand that plays well multi-way against a wide SB opening range. Heads-up vs a 2x open, I called preflop with A♥9♦. Flop comes 4♥4♣4♦ — a very dry, trip-board. 5-7 offsuit against a three-of-a-kind flop (4-4-4) is an easy fold. I have no hand, no draw, and virtually zero equity. Minni's raise into this board texture suggests a strong hand. Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) K♥4♠ is marginal but perfectly playable in position. Calling the 100 additional chips with Q-10 offsuit. Heads-up dynamics favor playing a wider range, I'm getting 3:1 odds, and Q-10 is in a good spot - too playable to fold, but marginal enough not to 3-bet. I'll see the flop from BB and make decisions based on the run-out.

I have a pair of tens with a Queen kicker on the flop (Kc-Tc-3d). This is a solid made hand but not a monster. The King on the board presents some concerns as it could have hit Minni's range. I opened preflop and hold K♥4♠ — top pair with a weak kicker on a fairly dry Kc Tc 3d flop. In position I should c‑bet to get value from worse Kx, Tx, small pairs and to deny free cards. Calling with pair of tens and Queen kicker. Getting 3:1 pot odds (200 to win 800) which are mathematically sound for a marginal made hand in heads-up play. Betting 400 on the turn with a pair of tens and Queen kicker. Minni checked, indicating relative weakness or board control. My pair is likely ahead of her checking range. I have K♥4♠ on K♣ T♣ 3♦ 9♠ — top pair with a weak kicker. Claude led for 400 into a 1200 pot (a small, ~25% pot probe). River check with pair of tens and queen kicker on a completed board Kc-Tc-3d-9s-8c. My pair of tens is a solid showdown hand but not dominant - it likely beats air and missed draws, but loses to overpairs (KK, QQ, JJ, 99), K-high hands, and the occasional made straight (if she had 7-6 or 6-5 type hands). Given that she called on the turn but the 9s didn't help drawing-heavy hands, checking captures the current value of my hand while minimizing downside risk. If she bets, I can make a call/fold decision with better information about her river betting pattern.

With deep stacks and a small pot, the right play is to control the pot and avoid a losing bluff — check back and conserve chips. If he bets I can fold; if he checks behind I’ll take my showdown (or muck) KQ high.

With 5-2 offsuit on the button, this is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm behind in chips (13,870 vs 25,980) and only need to call 50 more for 4:1 pot odds, 5-2o is still too weak to justify playing in this spot. With 30 hands remaining, I should preserve chips and wait for better opportunities where I can accumulate value. Folding weak hands from out-of-position preflop is a fundamental poker principle that I'll adhere to here.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) so my range should be wide. Q♦8♣ is a decent hand to open here — I want to take the initiative, build the pot in position, and apply fold equity. Calling 50 to complete would be passive and give Claude the lead postflop. A 3xBB open to 300 is standard: it exerts pressure, keeps the pot a manageable size relative to our deep stacks, and leaves room to fold to a large 3-bet. If Claude folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I have position and can C-bet many flops or control the pot. Versus a small 3-bet I may defend; versus a large shove/3-bet I will usually fold Q8o.

Facing a button raise with 8-9 offsuit from the big blind. While 8-9o is a connector with some potential, it's a marginal hand out of position. Minni has shown an aggressive style, raising frequently from late position. With 30 hands remaining and only 200 more to call, I need to be selective about which hands I defend with in the BB, especially when out of position. 8-9o doesn't have sufficient equity or playability to justify the call here. Folding preserves chips for stronger spots ahead. The hand has too much downside and too little upside at this stage of the tournament.

7-4 offsuit is statistically the worst starting hand in poker. While I'm already invested 50 chips in the small blind and the additional cost to call is only 50 more, this hand is far too weak to play even with the small additional investment required. With 28 hands remaining in the tournament and a reasonable stack (13.7K), I should preserve my chips for higher-quality hands. 7-4o will be dominated against Minni's range and is unlikely to win without significant help. Folding is the mathematically sound decision despite the small call investment.

Heads-up on the button with 4♠6♥: hand is marginal but in heads-up play the button should be wide and take the initiative. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; however opening to a standard 3xBB (to 300) achieves three things: (1) applies pressure to steal the BB, (2) keeps the initiative so I can C-bet many flops and play in position, and (3) is a size consistent with my normal open-raise sizing (harder for him to exploit). I’m happy to fold to a sizeable 3-bet, but if he calls I can continue-barrel many flops or use position to control the pot. Raising to 300 preserves stack flexibility while maximizing fold equity and range advantage heads-up.

In heads-up play with A7o, 3-betting the button raise is appropriate. This hand is well above the folding threshold in HU. I'm raising to 800 total to apply pressure, build the pot with a decent hand, and gain initiative from the button. Minni has shown frequent button raises throughout the session, suggesting a wide range I can exploit. A 2.7x 3-bet sizing is standard and balanced.

I opened wide from the button but Claude’s 3-bet to 800 is a meaningful raise. 4♠6♥ is very weak and frequently dominated by a standard 3-bet range; calling 500 to win the current ~1100 pot requires ~31% equity, which this hand doesn’t have. In position isn’t enough to overcome the poor equity and I’m better preserving my deep stack for better spots, so I fold.

In heads-up play on the button, positional advantage is critical. K6o is a weak hand, but button play in heads-up requires a significantly wider opening range than typical full-ring poker. I need to apply pressure and take down blinds frequently to maintain chip equity against Minni's larger stack (25,880 vs 13,970). A standard 2.5x BB raise (250 total) is reasonable here - it's aggressive but not suspiciously large, making it more likely to win the pot preflop while maintaining fold equity. Given my recent success with aggressive button plays (Hand #123), continuing this aggression is sound strategy.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a standard button open to 250 (2.5x BB). Pot is 350 and I need to put in 150 more — 250 total committed including my posted BB — so I’m getting ~30% break-even equity (150/(350+150)≈30%). T♠5♥ is a marginal hand but has decent heads‑up equity and backdoor possibilities against a wide BTN opening range. 3‑betting with T5o is a poor use of chips (hand is easily dominated and I’d be out of position postflop), so the best play is to defend and see a flop. I’ll call to realize equity and plan to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss, or continue when I pair tens or pick up good draws.

OOP heads‑up on a dry, paired flop (7d 7c 6h). I’ve got Ten‑of‑Spades + Five‑of‑Hearts (T5o) — no pair, only a remote backdoor straight (needs both an 8 and 9). Claude opened wide preflop so his range contains a lot of missed overcards but also many pairs and 7x that already beat me. With a small pot (500) and deep stacks, I don’t want to build a big pot out of position with a marginal hand and no useful blockers. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets to see a turn (I’ll tolerate ~<=1/3–1/2 pot bets depending on sizing) but fold to heavy/committing aggression. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only invest further when I pair or pick up a clear draw.

K6o hit the flop and made a pair of 6s with K kicker. The board shows 77-6, and Minni checked to me. This is a good opportunity to apply pressure with a hand that has decent showdown value. A c-bet here is standard - I want to take down the pot immediately or extract value from weaker hands. Sizing at 280 (roughly 56% of pot) is aggressive enough to apply real pressure while maintaining fold equity. Given my stack situation, I need to stay aggressive in heads-up play to maintain chip equity against Minni's bigger stack.

Call. Preflop pot was 500 and Claude's c-bet of 280 makes the pot 780; calling 280 requires I have 530 committed total (250 preflop + 280). My break-even equity on this call is ~26% (280 / (780+280) ≈ 26.4%). T5s has limited but nonzero equity (pair outs + backdoor straight possibilities) and against a wide CO/button open + standard c-bet this is roughly in the calling range. I don't want to inflate the pot out of position or bluff-raise without useful blockers, so I’ll defend cheaply to see a turn and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear draw.

Check. I called the flop to realize equity against a wide open-raising range; the turn Ace is a bad card for my holding (T5o) because it heavily improves Claude’s range (AX, A7/A6, sets) and reduces my bluff/semibluff equity. Pot is only 1,060 and stacks are deep (me 25,450 / him 13,490) — no reason to build the pot out of position with nothing. Betting here as a bluff has poor risk/reward (I lack an Ace-blocker and won’t get many worse hands to fold). Plan: keep the pot small by checking; if Claude barrels the river I’ll fold absent a meaningful improvement (pair or clear draw) and only invest further when I have real equity or very favorable odds.

The Ace on the turn is a concerning card that likely improved Minni's range significantly. My K6 (pair of 6s) has showdown value but is likely not the best hand. With a shorter stack than Minni, I should avoid bloating the pot with marginal holdings. Checking allows me to see the river while controlling risk - if she bets the river, I can evaluate based on the final board. This is a prudent decision given the uncertainty around her hand strength and the turn's card texture.

Heads‑up on a 7♦7♣6♥ A♦ T♦ runout — I hold T♠5♥, which turned into second pair on the river. Claude opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop (I called) and both checked the turn. His checked river range contains a lot of missed over‑cards and medium hands (Kx/Qx/Jx, weaker pocket pairs) that I beat, but also A× and the occasional set (77/66) that crush me. Checking behind surrenders thin value; betting extracts value from the weaker part of his range while a small size reduces the chance of a big check‑raise by his strongest hands. I’ll make a constructive value bet of about one‑third pot (≈350 into 1060) to get called by worse hands. If he check‑raises substantially I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack.

Calling 350 to win 1,410 provides 4:1 pot odds. While my K6o (pair of 6s) is weak on this 7-7-6-A-T board, the modest river bet sizing and Minni's turn check suggest this may not be a monster hand. Short-stacked play requires taking favorable mathematical spots—I have reasonable showdown value and there's genuine equity against her range of bluffs or medium hands. The pot odds justify the call even with a marginal holding.

Heads-up on the button I should be aggressive and open a very wide range. 3♦8♥ is weak but playable in position — raising for initiative gains fold equity and lets me play postflop with position. Min-raise to 2BB (200 total) is standard here: it pressures the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and preserves options postflop. If Claude 3-bets big I'll fold; if he calls I'll use position to C-bet favorable flops or realize equity quietly.

Folding 2-5o from the big blind. While the pot odds of 3:1 are mathematically acceptable, 2-5 is the weakest hand in poker and will be very difficult to play profitably postflop even with position. At this critical stage (125/150 hands), with balanced stacks, I should wait for better opportunities rather than defend blindly with trash hands against an aggressive opponent. Minni will continue raising frequently, creating more spots to pick my battles.

Raising to 250 from button with 2-J offsuit in heads-up play. While this is a weak holding, position and the need to maintain chip velocity in heads-up justify applying pressure. The raise is not excessive relative to stack sizes (about 2.5x BB), allowing me to win the blind frequently while remaining exploitable if called. This balances aggression with caution - standard button strategy that exploits positional advantage without overcommitting weak equity.

I’m in the big blind with K♣7♠ facing a standard button/SB raise to 250. To call is 150 more (250 total) into a 350 pot — calling gives me ~30% equity requirement (150 into a 500 pot), which is reasonable heads-up against a wide button opening range. K7o is marginal but playable heads-up and I prefer to realize equity OOP rather than inflate the pot with a marginal hand by min-raising to 400. I’ll call and see a flop: if I hit (K,7, good draw) I’ll play aggressively; if I miss and face heavy aggression I’ll fold to avoid bloating the pot out of position. If Claude shoves I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold depending on shove size and read).

Heads-up on a dry K-9-3 flop and I have top pair (K♣7♠) out of position. Claude opened a wide button range preflop, so I’m ahead of plenty of hands (weaker Kx, many 9x, small pairs, and floats). I want to extract value and deny free cards to hands that can catch up on the turn/river, while keeping worse hands in. A bet of ~60% pot (~300 into 500) charges worse kings and pairs, protects my hand and builds the pot when I’m likely ahead. If Claude raises I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing, but this bet is the right default to maximize value and fold equity on this board.

I raised pre-flop from the button with a marginal 2-J offsuit and was called by Minni from the big blind. On a K-9-3 flop, I've whiffed completely with no pair, no draw, and minimal equity. Minni's raise to 300 into a 500-chip pot suggests strength, and I have no realistic hand to continue with. While heads-up play requires aggression, this situation calls for discipline. With only 24 hands remaining and stack management critical, bluffing or calling out of position with ace-high at best against an opponent who has demonstrated strength is poor risk-reward. Folding here preserves chips for better spots where I can leverage my positional advantage more effectively.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with pocket threes (3♦3♠). In heads-up play you should open a very wide range; 3s have set-mining value and play well in position. I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and keep the preflop size small enough to preserve implied odds to hit a set. Raise to the standard 2×BB (200 total) — if Claude calls I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (call smaller 3-bets for set equity, fold to very large overbets).

Raising with QQ in heads-up. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively, especially when out of position. A 3x raise builds the pot, establishes strength, and puts Minni to a decision. With a short stack relative to Minni, I need to take profitable spots, and QQ vs his button-raising range is exactly that kind of spot.

Claude 3-bet to 600 after my 200 open. I have pocket 3s (3♦3♠) in heads-up with position — a classic set-mining/call spot. The immediate pot odds for the call are 400/(800+400)=33.3%, which is worse than my raw chance to flop a set (~11.8%) or make trips by river (~19%). However the effective stack (Claude ~12.2k) is deep, so implied odds are very strong: if I hit a set I can often extract a large payoff. Claude’s 3-bet sizing is standard (not crushingly large) and his heads-up range will include both value and bluffs, which also supports calling to see a flop in position. I will not 4‑bet here — 3s are poor 4‑bet hands and I want to keep him in to realize implied odds. Postflop plan: if I miss I’ll play cautiously (check/fold to heavy pressure on scary boards, or check-call small C-bets on dry boards); if I hit a set or a favourable board I’ll extract value. Call to 600 total (400 more).

Betting QQ on Jd Td 5h. I have an overpair to a dry flop after raising pre-flop. A c-bet of 500 (approximately 40% pot) is standard value extraction. This bet is consistent with my pre-flop aggression, builds the pot when I'm likely ahead, and applies pressure on Minni's range. Stack depth is good at 12,190, so I can continue playing the hand with flexibility on subsequent streets.

I have pocket 3s and missed this J♦ T♦ 5♥ flop. Claude 3‑bet preflop, so his continuing range is weighted toward strong hands, but his flop sizing (500 into a 1700 pot) is small and can include a lot of probes/bluffs. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and allows me to realize equity (sometimes I’m already ahead vs air/overcards and I still have two outs to hit trips by the river). Immediate pot odds on the call are ~22.7% (500/(1700+500)); hitting a set by river is only ~8.4%, so this isn’t a pure set‑mine — I’m calling to use position and to fold to genuine turn aggression if I don’t improve. I won’t raise: 3s are a poor bluff‑raise hand on this two‑diamond, high‑card board and a raise will unnecessarily bloat the pot against a strong 3‑better. Plan: call 500, re‑evaluate on the turn (check/call small bets, fold to large pressure; value/size up if I hit trips).

Betting 1,100 on the turn with QQ. The King is not a particularly threatening card for my overpair in this dynamic. I have QQ which is still strong, and I should maintain aggression to build the pot and apply pressure. This bet size (roughly 50% of pot) is standard for value extraction and consistent with my established aggressive line. My stack is deep enough to continue playing flexibly on the river.

Fold. I have pocket threes on J♦ T♦ 5♥ K♠ after Claude 3‑bet preflop, small flop c‑bet and now a 1,100 turn bet into the ~2,200 pot (pot shown 3,300, to call 1,100). Calling 1,100 to win a final pot of ~4,400 gives ~25% pot odds (1,100/4,400). I only have two outs to make a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35% equity) and no realistic backdoor draws — the turn K♠ removed the diamond backdoor. Claude’s line is very consistent with value (Kx, Jx, TT, sets, or Q9 straights) given the preflop 3‑bet and continued betting, so my raw equity and implied odds don’t justify a call. A bluff‑raise is unattractive: I lack relevant blockers and he’s likely to call or shove with many better hands. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a +EV spot — fold here.

With J7o on the button in heads-up play at 128 of 150 hands, I need to apply pressure while my stack is still reasonable (13,840). J7 is a marginal hand but playable from the button. Rather than calling passively or folding, raising to 250 chips (2.5x the big blind) applies aggression and gives me the initiative. This betting pattern has been working well in recent hands - forcing Minni to make decisions post-flop where he's been vulnerable at times. If he folds, I gain chips; if he calls, I still have some showdown equity and can control the action. The raise size is proportional to the pot and my hand strength, avoiding the desperation of an all-in while maintaining my short stack value.

Heads-up vs a button open to 250 — their opening range is wide. J♥7♦ is marginal but has decent heads-up equity and good playability postflop, so I want to defend my big blind without bloating the pot preflop. A small 3-bet here is unnecessary and can commit me with a weak hand; folding is too tight. I will call the 150 more to see a flop (total committed 250). Postflop plan: evaluate texture — proceed aggressively if I hit (top pair or two pair) or when I have clear fold equity; otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy action.

Flop: Qc Qh 9c. I hold Jh7d — no pair, no club, only a very slim backdoor heart route. Claude opened wide from the button, but this texture heavily favors his range (lots of Qx and club combos). On a paired board with two clubs my fold equity is low and betting would often get called by better hands or club draws, putting me in a tough spot out of position. With deep stacks and a small pot, I prefer pot control: check and reevaluate. Plan: fold to a sizable continuation bet; consider a call only to very small probes if pot odds and villain tendencies justify it; if Claude checks back, re-evaluate on the turn and only become aggressive when I pick up real equity (a J/7 or a runout that disfavors his range).

On the flop with J7o against a paired board (QcQhQc9c), I need to exercise caution. While I raised pre-flop for position and aggression, this flop is unfavorable for my hand strength. J7 has minimal equity against Minni's likely range - if he has a queen, I'm crushed; if he doesn't, he still has overcards and the nine. Minni's check suggests weakness, but I'm short-stacked at 13,640 chips with 22 hands remaining. Betting here would burn chips on marginal equity. Checking allows me to see another community card before committing further, potentially make a better decision on the turn with two cards revealed, and preserve my chips for stronger spots. This is a pragmatic approach given my chip position and remaining hands.

Board Qc Qh 9c 4h, I hold J♥7♦ — no pair and almost no realistic equity. With only one card to come I can only make a single pair (Js or 7s); a flush is impossible from this spot. Claude’s checked the flop and the turn — his range still heavily contains Qx, pocket pairs and club combos that dominate me. Betting here as a bluff has poor fold equity and will often just get called by better hands, so I want to keep the pot small. Action: check and re-evaluate the river; fold to any substantial bet on the turn, call only very small probes that the pot odds justify, and if I pair a J or 7 on the river I’ll look to extract value.

Minni has shown clear weakness with back-to-back checks on a paired board (QQ9). My J7 has minimal showdown equity - it's unlikely to win at showdown and doesn't connect with the board. Given my short stack (13,640 with 22 hands remaining), I need to build chips opportunistically. This is a spot where applying pressure makes sense: if Minni folds, I win the pot; if he calls, I'll see the river and make a decision with more information. The bet sizing of 350 is roughly 70% of the big blind - enough to apply meaningful pressure without over-committing my stack. Checking down to the river with J-high against an opponent who could be holding anything is inefficient. The bet exploits his weakness and gives me the best chance to take down this pot now rather than lose at showdown.

I fold. On Qc Qh 9c 4h with Jh7d I have no pair and essentially only six clean outs (three J, three 7) to improve on the river — roughly 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Claude's turn bet is 350 into a 850 pot, so calling 350 to win 1200 requires about 350/(850+350) ≈ 29.2% equity to break even. My ~13% equity is far below that. His line (check-check on the flop then a turn bet) is consistent with lots of value-heavy holdings (trip Q, sets, 9x, medium pairs) that dominate me, so I can't expect enough bluffs to make a call +EV. Raising as a bluff is also unattractive: I have little fold equity versus his likely thin value range and would be risking a large portion of my stack with a very weak hand. With a healthy chip lead, preserving chips and folding marginal spots is the correct strategy.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 8♦7♥ — a playable, connected hand in position. The pot is 150 and a simple call is only 50, but I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to Claude’s wide BB defending range. I’ll open to 300 (≈3BB) to gain fold equity, keep the pot size reasonable given deep stacks, and play postflop in position. Plan: if he 3-bets large I’ll usually concede this spot with 8-7o; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable low/connected boards and control the pot on high, dry boards, extracting value when I hit straights or two-pair/strong draws.

J7o is one of the weakest starting hands. Although I have good pot odds (2:1) and deep stacks, being out of position against an aggressive opponent with such a weak hand makes this a clear fold. Position and hand strength are critical factors in No-Limit Hold'em, and this spot fails on both counts. Better opportunities will present themselves in the remaining 21 hands.

Raising to 250 total with AJh from the button. This is a strong premium hand in heads-up that warrants aggression. I apply pressure to take down the blinds immediately or play a strong hand post-flop with positional advantage. My chip deficit (13,990 vs 25,860) with 20 hands remaining justifies an aggressive stance with quality holdings. AJ suited is a profitable raise in this spot against most reasonable opponents.

6♣2♥ is essentially junk: unsuited, disconnected, and frequently dominated. Claude opened to 250; the pot is 350 and I must call 150, which gives me 150/(350+150)=30% pot odds (need ~30% equity to break even). Versus a reasonable heads‑up button opening range my actual equity with 6‑2o is well below that and I’m often crushed or with poor playability postflop. 3‑betting to 400 as a bluff is a bad use of chips here — I’d be out of position postflop and have little fold equity. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♣T♥ — a strong, playable hand in position. The call is only 50 but I want to take the initiative, build the pot with position, and apply pressure to Claude’s wide defending range. A standard heads-up open of ~2.5–3x the BB is appropriate, so I’ll raise to 300 total. If Claude folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I have position and can c-bet many flops or realize equity. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding or calling depending on sizing).

7-3 offsuit is the worst hand in poker and should be folded from all positions. While Minni has shown aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament (raising frequently), my 7-3 has virtually no equity against any reasonable hand. With 20 hands remaining and a healthy 140BB stack, there's no justification to call with this hand. I should preserve my chips for premium hands where I can apply aggressive pressure. Even against a wide raising range, 7-3 lacks any playability - it can't make strong straights, has weak kicker problems, and will struggle in any confrontation.

Tc2h is one of the worst hands in poker. Despite favorable button position and reasonable pot odds, the hand has virtually no value and cannot be played profitably. With 19 hands remaining and slightly short-stacked (~14k), preserving chips and waiting for premium hands is the correct strategic approach. Folding weak holdings from any position maximizes expected value over the tournament's final stretch.

Heads-up on the button with A♣2♥ — a hand with a big blocker and good postflop playability. Pot is 150 and it’s standard to apply pressure from the button rather than limp. Min raise is 200, but I’ll open to a normal 3xBB sizing (300 total) to get fold equity, isolate the BB, and leverage position. If Claude folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I keep position with a hand that plays well multi-way heads-up; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies.

Folding A4o in the BB vs button raise. While I have reasonable pot odds, the hand is too weak out of position against an aggressive player. With a chip disadvantage (35% of Minni's stack) and only 17 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve chips for premium hands where I have better equity. A4o will miss the flop 70% of the time and even when it hits, the weak kicker is vulnerable in multiway or when facing aggression.

Heads-up on the button with K♥2♥. In 2-player poker, button play is significantly looser than full-ring positions. While K2o is weak in absolute terms, positional advantage post-flop makes this a profitable call. With only 50 more chips needed to see the flop and position secured, calling is standard heads-up strategy. This allows me to play a wider range and control post-flop action while being able to fold to aggressive play when needed.

Claude limped the button, which is a very wide/weak range heads-up. I hold 8♣7♥ offsuit — a speculative, marginal hand that wants to see a cheap flop, especially deep-stacked (~260 bb). Raising from the big blind would bloat the pot and leave me out of position postflop against a wide calling range; a small raise also invites a reraise. I’ll check and see the flop for free (or nearly free). Plan: continue on favorable flops (pair, straight/runner-runner draws) and fold to heavy aggression on dry misses, preserving stack for better spots.

Board K♠-3♦-5♦; I hold 8♣7♥ — I completely missed and have no diamond/straight draw. Claude limped the button, so his range is very wide and contains lots of Kx, small pairs and diamond draws that will call a c-bet. I’m out of position and stacks are very deep (~260bb), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal bluff that will often get called. Plan: pot control — check and fold to heavy aggression, possibly call a very small stab for pot odds but otherwise preserve chips for better spots.

Top pair (K♥ on K♠3♦5♦) in heads-up position after opponent's flop check. Betting for value and protection against draws. Position and weak opponent action justify aggressive play. Standard flop c-bet sizing builds pot while keeping commitment reasonable.

I checked the flop with 8♣7♥ and completely missed K♠-3♦-5♦. Claude limp‑called pre and now makes a small flop stab to 120 into a 320 pot — a fairly wide, low‑size c‑bet heads‑up. I have only 6 clean outs (≈24% to pair by river) so pure draw equity is slightly below the pot odds (call 120 to win ~440 → ~27%). However his limp→small‑bet range is wide and contains plenty of hands I can outplay on later streets. Raising OOP with nothing would bloat the pot and is unnecessary. Plan: keep the pot controlled, call the small bet to see a turn and re‑evaluate — fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve or can credibly bluff later.

Heads‑up vs a very wide limp/call button range. I called Claude’s small flop stab with 8♣7♥ intending to control the pot; the turn K♦ now pairs the board and significantly improves a lot of his continuing hands (many Kx and turned boat/diamond combos). I have no diamond and no pair (only ~6 outs to pair by river, ~24% equity), so bluffing into a range that contains many kings is too risky with deep stacks. Pot control is optimal: check and fold to meaningful aggression, maybe call a tiny probe but never bloat the pot. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river, but I won’t barrel without improvement or a very specific fold equity line.

Three of a kind Kings with 5-3 kickers on a K♠ 3♦ 5♦ K♦ board. Minni checked the turn, indicating weakness. This is a premium hand that needs to extract value. Betting 250 chips (57% of pot) keeps the pot building while remaining exploitatively thin enough to encourage calls from marginal holdings. In heads-up play, aggressive value betting from position with strong hands is essential for stack accumulation. Final card coming on river.

Claude’s line (limp/call pre, small 120 flop c‑bet, now 250 on a K‑pairing turn) strongly suggests Kx, a pair, or other hands that improved on the turn. I hold 8♣7♥ with no diamond and only 6 clean outs (three 8s + three 7s) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river. Calling 250 into the effective pot (690 + 250 bet, and my 250 call) means I’m risking 250 to win ~1,190, so I need ≈21% equity to justify a pure call. That’s well above my actual equity. Raising as a bluff OOP with no blockers is too risky and likely to bloat a pot I’m not flipping in. With deep stacks and a line that heavily favors Claude, the lowest-variance, +EV choice is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦2♣ — a weak holding, but in heads-up play position and initiative are more valuable than the absolute hand strength. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to act; rather than passively calling, I want to seize initiative, build fold equity, and be able to play postflop in position. A standard open to the minimum legal raise (200 total) applies pressure to the BB, keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand, and lets me c-bet many flops. If Claude 3-bets big I can fold and preserve my deeper stack; if he calls I can exploit position postflop. Raise to 200.

Reraising to 450 with AX in heads-up play. This is a standard aggression move that: (1) shows strength to apply pressure on Minni, (2) builds a pot where my strong hand is ahead of many of Minni's raising range, (3) gives me positional advantage postflop by having already shown aggression, and (4) is appropriate stack sizing given my chip count of 14,010. Ace-high is a strong hand in late tournament play, and I want to take down the blinds or get value if Minni continues with a weaker holding.

Claude 3-bet to 450 after my open to 200. The pot is 650 and it only costs me 250 more (450 total) to continue — break-even equity ≈ 250/900 = 27.8%. Heads‑up and in position I can realize equity postflop and exploit a fairly wide 3‑bet range. Q♦2♣ is weak, but the price is cheap, stacks are deep (large implied odds) and 4‑betting would be an unnecessary polarization with a poor hand. I will call to keep the pot manageable, use position to play postflop, c‑bet or fold to heavy action depending on the board texture.

Continuation bet on the flop after reraising preflop. With Ad8c on 3c-4c-3s, I have ace-high which is vulnerable but ahead of many hands in Minni's calling range. The paired board is relatively dry and safe for my range. In heads-up play at this stage (hand 135 of 150), maintaining aggressive pressure is critical. A bet of ~400 chips (45% of the 900 pot) is standard sizing that shows strength without overcommitting, keeping good pot control.

Claude 3‑bet preflop and now fires a small probe (400 into a 1,300 pot). Qd2c has no pair but a backdoor club and a queen overcard — in position I can realize equity and use post‑flop information. The flop bet is small so his range is wide (continuation bets, some pairs, some single‑pair/trips combos, and air) and I get ~23.5% pot odds to call (400 into 1,700). A bluff‑raise here with Q2 is too thin against a 3‑bettor and a fold surrenders too much given the price and our positional advantage. I will call 400 to see the turn, float hands that miss and look to take the pot on favorable turns (Q, club or a checked turn), but re‑evaluate/fold to strong turn aggression if I miss.

Turn Ace gives me a pair of Aces with an 8 kicker, which is very strong in this heads-up matchup. Given Minni's calling line through the flop, I'm likely ahead of their hand range. In late tournament heads-up play (16 hands left), I need to extract value from stronger hands and apply pressure. A 750 chip bet (~44% pot) achieves this balance, building value while maintaining good fold equity against complete misses. This bet sizing also avoids overcommitting while getting paid off by weaker Aces or smaller pairs.

Claude 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet on the two‑club, paired flop and now led again on the turn (A♣). He’s representing a wide range (A‑x, pairs, some clubs, bluffs). Pot is 2,450 and it costs 750 to call, so break‑even equity = 750/(2,450+750) ≈ 23.4%. With Q♦2♣ I have a one‑card flush draw to the clubs (9 club outs) plus pair outs (Qx and small 2x) — unique outs ≈ 14/46 → raw equity ≈ 30.4%. Some outs are “dirty” (pair outs can still lose to A or sets; some club outs could give him a better flush if he holds two clubs), but even discounted equity is around or above the break‑even threshold. Raising as a bluff is too thin with Q2; folding surrenders too much given the price and my positional advantage. I’ll call 750 to see the river, then re‑evaluate: if I hit the flush or a clean Q I can extract value; if the river blanks I’ll fold to large aggression.

River value bet with AK high. My hand is extremely strong and Minni's calling line suggests a weaker holding. The Kh on the river is a favorable runout card for my range. Betting ~1200 (roughly 37% of pot) gets value from worse Aces, pairs, and failed draws while keeping proper pot control. In the final 16 hands of tournament, extracting value from strong hands is critical to chip accumulation. This bet has good balance - strong enough to get called by worse, but not so large that I'm only called by premium holdings.

Fold. The river (3c 4c 3s Ac Kh) leaves me with Q♦2♣ — no pair and no flush; I missed my outs. Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet and continued aggression on flop/turn/river) is heavily weighted to value (Ax, Kx, sets/full houses, made clubs), so the small river bet is more likely thin value than a frequent bluff. Calling 1,200 into a 4,400 pot requires ~21% equity; I have essentially no showdown equity here, and a raise would be an overly thin bluff with poor blockers. I’ll fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

Folding 9-3 from the small blind. While position is strong in heads-up play, this hand is bottom-tier and lacks playability. With 15,660 chips and 14 hands remaining, there's no reason to get involved in marginal spots. Better hands will come against Minni.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 8♦9♥ — a very playable, connected hand in heads-up where ranges are wide. I want to take the initiative and build the pot with position postflop, so I’ll raise to the standard 2xBB sizing. Raising to 200 total applies pressure to the big blind, extracts value from worse hands, and gives me the lead to c-bet favorable flops; if Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand, but I’ll continue against a call and proceed with aggressive c-bets on wet/connected flops and pot control on dry overcard boards.

Raising to 550 total chips with AJ offsuit. This is a strong premium hand that warrants aggressive play, especially in response to Minni's min-raise from the button which often signals weakness. The raise of 450 more (after calling the 100 already owed) applies appropriate pressure while maintaining reasonable stack management given my short-stacked position at 15,560. In heads-up play, I need to build pots with premium hands and take advantage of weak aggression from the opponent.

Claude 3‑bet my 200 open to 550. I hold 8♦9♥ — a playable, connected offsuit hand in heads‑up but still marginal versus a 3‑bet. With position and deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot manageable: calling 350 more (550 total) lets me realize equity and play post‑flop aggressively on favorable boards while folding to heavy action on dry, high‑card flops. A small 4‑bet would bloat the pot with a hand that’s often dominated and doesn’t gain much fold equity, so I’ll call and evaluate the flop in position.

Betting 450 chips on the Kd 6h 3d flop with AJ offsuit. This continues my aggressive pre-flop narrative and applies pressure to Minni's range. Her initial min-raise suggests weakness, and a decisive bet here forces her to make a decision. With a short stack, I need to build pots with reasonable hands and leverage aggression. The bet size is roughly pot-sized and maintains good stack-to-pot ratio management.

I’m out of position preflop but have position on the flop with 8♦9♥ on K♦ 6♥ 3♦. I have no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond (would need runner-runner) and 6 clean outs to make a pair (~24% to hit by the river). The pot is 1550 and Claude bet 450; calling 450 to potentially win ~2450 requires roughly 18.4% equity, so the pot odds justify a call. Raising as a bluff is too thin versus a 3‑bettor and would bloat the pot with little fold equity; folding would give up +EV pot odds and discard position. I’ll call 450, plan to evaluate the turn (continue with a pair or strong draw, float and apply pressure on favorable turns, fold to heavy continued aggression if I miss).

Top pair (Ace-Jack) on the turn after hitting the Ace. I've been aggressive pre-flop and flop, and this card is excellent for my range. Minni called the flop, suggesting a medium-strength hand. With limited stack size (14,660), I need to build pots with strong hands. Betting around 40% of the pot continues my aggression narrative while maintaining stack efficiency. This is a value bet that should get called by worse hands and likely induces folds from weaker holdings, while also protecting against any draw possibilities.

Line recap: I opened, Claude 3‑bet preflop and has led every street (bet flop and now bet turn). I hold 8♦9♥ on K♦ 6♥ 3♦ / A♥ — I missed the turn and only have 6 clean outs (three 8s + three 9s) to make a pair on the river. That's ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Pot = 2750 and Claude's turn bet is 750; calling 750 to win 3500 requires ~21.4% equity (750/3500). My raw equity (≈13%) is well below the pot‑odds threshold, so a call is -EV. A raise to 1500 as a bluff is too risky against a player who 3‑bet preflop and barreled twice — he will often continue with Kx/Ax/top pairs and I lack fold equity or meaningful equity if called. I’ll fold, preserve a big stack, and wait for a better spot in later hands.

Folding 4-7 offsuit from the small blind. Despite heads-up play encouraging wider ranges and 3:1 odds, this hand is simply too weak. It lacks connectivity, high cards, and straight potential. My stack is healthy enough to wait for better opportunities, and discipline with weak hands is crucial for long-term success.

Heads-up on the button with J♦4♠ and deep stacks (~233bb). J4o is a weak hand but position is extremely valuable in heads-up play — we should open a wide range to take the initiative and collect the blind or play postflop in position. Make a standard open-raise to 200 total (2x effective) to apply pressure and keep the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this marginal holding, but if Claude calls we’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry boards.

Calling with Ad3h at 3:1 pot odds in heads-up play. Any ace has reasonable equity against Minni's wide button-raising range, and the pot odds are attractive. With 12 hands remaining and healthy stacks, this is a standard call that allows me to see a flop and play the hand postflop.

Checking with Ad3h on Jh 8s 5c. I have ace-high, which is vulnerable and best played cautiously. Out of position, it makes sense to let Minni act first. This also keeps the pot size manageable with limited cards remaining in the tournament. A check-call approach is more profitable here than betting my marginal holding.

Flop is Jh 8s 5c — I have top pair (J♦) with a very weak 4 kicker. Heads-up and in position this is a clear betting spot: the board is dry (no obvious straight/flush) and Claude checked, so a continuation bet will get value from worse Jx, many pocket pairs, and will take the pot away from pure air. I want to extract value while keeping the pot manageable given the weak kicker, so I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (200). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue when the turn is a blank; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to stronger two-pair/sets or better jacks and fold most of the time (unless the sizing/read suggests a light bluff).

Calling with Ad3h on Jh 8s 5c. Pot odds of 3:1 are favorable for an ace-high hand in heads-up play. With only 12 hands remaining and similar stacks, I should be playing most ace-high hands in this spot. My ace has decent equity against Minni's wide range and reasonable outs to improve. The marginal hand is playable given the odds and position coming on future streets.

With Ad3h on Jh 8s 5c 8c turn, I have ace-high with limited improvement chances. The pairing of the turn 8 makes the board more dangerous for my holdings. Minni checked the turn, which I can exploit by controlling the pot size rather than forcing a decision. In heads-up with only 12 hands remaining, keeping the pot manageable allows me to see the river and reassess. Checking also prevents Minni from folding weak hands preemptively. My ace still has showdown value, and ace-high may win if I can see the river cheaply. This is a check-through strategy that maximizes my chances of either winning a small pot or folding to a river bet if Minni shows strength.

Turn paired (8c) — this is NOT the “blank” I planned to barreI on. I have top pair (J♦) with a very weak 4 kicker. Claude called my flop c‑bet and now checks the paired turn; his check is consistent with either a slow‑played 8 (two‑pair/set), a medium Jx pot‑control line, or a wide call on flop. There are almost no realistic draws to protect against (a club flush on the turn would require two clubs in his hand), so my priority is pot control and avoiding getting exploited by a check‑raise or a big river shove. I’ll check back the turn to keep the pot manageable, see a free/cheap river, and re‑evaluate: call small/medium river bets for value with top pair, but fold to large river aggression that strongly represents two‑pair/set.

Checking with Ad3h on the river (Jh 8s 5c 8c Th) makes sense for several reasons: (1) My ace-high is too weak to bet for value, and likely loses to most hands that call; (2) Minni's turn check suggests they may not have a premium holding; (3) With only 12 hands left and similar stacks (~16k vs ~23k), I should preserve chips by taking marginal hands to showdown rather than creating pots I'll likely lose; (4) Checking allows me to see the outcome without risking additional chips on a bluff or value bet that's unlikely to work. If Minni bets the river, I can make a fold/call decision based on the specific amount and my sense of their play pattern.

Turn check-back controlled the pot after Claude called my flop c-bet. River T completes some hands that now beat me (any T, and any 8x that turned into trips), but I still have top pair and can get value from worse Jx, small pairs and some A-high hands that will call a small bet. I'll make a small blocking/value bet of 200 (≈1/4 pot) to extract thin value and charge hands that want a cheap showdown while keeping the commitment low. If Claude raises, his range is heavily weighted to hands that beat me (two-pair/trips/sets or a very strong jack), so I will fold to significant river aggression.

Calling with Ad3h on the river. Minni's turn check and small river bet (200 into 1000) suggest weakness. Against their likely range of checked-back hands, my ace-high has significant equity (30-40%). The 5:1 pot odds are excellent—I only need 16.7% equity to call profitably. In heads-up play with 11 hands remaining and close stacks, this is a standard call that allows me to win pots I have reasonable equity in. The small bet sizing pattern indicates Minni is unlikely to have a strong hand, making this mathematically sound.

Folding J♥2♥ from the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with minimal equity. With only 10 hands left and a short stack, preservation and selectivity are key. Better to fold weak hands and wait for premium spots rather than play marginal holdings from poor positions.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K6 of hearts — a playable, suited king that does well as a button opener. With deep stacks (~240bb) and only one opponent, I want the initiative and to deny a cheap flop. Standard heads-up sizing of 3xBB puts pressure and builds pots with position; I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so +250). If called I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet favorable textures; if Claude 3-bets big I can fold and preserve stack.

QcJc is a premium hand in heads-up play. With 10 hands remaining and slightly shorter stack, I'm 3-betting to approximately 2.5x Minni's raise. This accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) takes down pot immediately in some spots where Minni folds with weak holdings, (2) builds pot with strong hand where I can have advantage, (3) applies pressure on the button from the big blind to establish that I'm not playing passively. Minni has shown a wide-range button play pattern throughout the tournament, making this a good spot to assert control with a strong hand.

I opened to 300 from the button with K♥6♥ and Claude 3‑bet to 900. Heads‑up and deep‑stacked, K6s is a hand I want to see a flop with in position — it has suitedness, king blockers and reasonable equity against a wide 3‑bet range. Pot is ~1200 and I need to call 600 more (900 total), giving me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). A 4‑bet to 1500 is too thin for value and can lead to uncomfortable all‑in spots; folding is too tight in heads‑up. I’ll call and use my position postflop: continue with strong/top‑pair/heart draws, c‑bet favorable textures, and check/realize or fold versus heavy resistance on dry boards.

Checking is the prudent play with QJ on a dry Ace-high flop when out of position and short-stacked. With 10 hands remaining, preserving chips and gathering information on the turn takes priority over aggressive continuation betting. This hand has equity potential (gutshot to K, two unpaired cards) but isn't strong enough to bet into an opponent who flatted my 3-bet and likely has either an Ace or medium pair. Checking allows me to see the turn and make informed decisions based on Minni's action, while keeping the pot manageable given my stack constraints.

Flop: A♥ 2♥ 3♠. I hold K♥6♥ — no pair but a very strong heart draw (four hearts including the A on board means I have the highest possible heart in hand). Heads‑up vs a preflop 3‑bettor, Claude’s check here doesn’t deny a wide range; betting gives me fold equity and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1200) as a semi‑bluff — it can take the pot now, folds out many hands with some showdown value, and gives correct odds to call a reasonable check‑raise because of the nut‑heart blocker and strong draw. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (press with a heart or favorable card, pot‑control or fold to heavy resistance on dry cards).

Calling 1,200 to see the turn with QJ on a dry Ace-high flop. I have reasonable equity with gutshot to the nuts plus two overcards against Minni's flatting range (estimated 35-45% equity). Pot odds of 2.5:1 justify the call mathematically. With 10 hands remaining and reasonable chip stack, seeing another card is appropriate rather than getting committed with a hand that needs to improve. Maintains flexibility to continue, check-fold, or value bet depending on turn card. Folding now would be giving up too easily with a strong unpaired hand that has significant improvement potential.

Checking with QJ high on a paired board (2h Ah 3s Ac). After calling down the flop, I have no made hand and am almost certainly behind Minni's continuing range (likely Ace or strong pair). With ~9 outs (Kings and clubs for flush draw) and only one card remaining, and with 10 hands left where chip preservation matters, checking to see the river for free is the prudent play. Betting commits more chips with a drawing hand that is almost certainly behind, wasting chips in a critical endgame stage. This also maintains the pot size given my slightly shorter stack relative to Minni.

I have K♥6♥ — a very strong heart draw (the highest heart blocker) on A♥ 2♥ 3♠. I semi‑bluffed the flop and was called; the turn (A♣) paired the board so Claude can have Ax/trips, some hearts or medium pairs, but he checked. With one card to come I have ~9 outs (~19.6%) to make a flush and my K♥ is the best kicker among single‑heart draws. Checking lets him see a free river and surrenders fold equity. I want to both build the pot when I hit and fold out weaker hands that called the flop, so I’ll continue as a semi‑bluff with a sizable but non‑committing bet — about 2/3 pot. If Claude calls I still have solid equity to a river heart and will re‑evaluate; if he check‑raises large I’ll fold (I won’t call big turn raises with just a draw).

Calling with QcJc on a 2h Ah 3s Ac board. I have 13 outs (10 clubs for flush + 3 non-club Kings for straight), giving me approximately 27.7% equity. With pot odds of 3.5:1 and break-even equity of 22.2%, this is a profitable +EV call. With 10 hands remaining in the tournament, taking mathematically sound spots with strong draws is essential for competing in the endgame. The draw is strong with two ways to improve, and folding would be surrendering equity incorrectly.

Checking with Queen-high on a paired Ace board. I have no made hand whatsoever - just queen-high which is virtually nothing on this texture. Minni has demonstrated strength on every street (betting flop and turn consistently), making his range heavily weighted toward strong holdings (pairs, Aces, etc.). Bluffing here lacks credibility and would needlessly burn chips in the critical 10-hand endgame. Checking allows me to see showdown without additional investment, and if Minni bets, I can make an informed fold/call decision based on pot odds. This preserves my 11060 chips for the remaining hands.

I rivered the nut heart: K♥6♥ on A♥ 2♥ 3♠ / A♣ / 4♥ gives me A‑K‑6‑4‑2 hearts — the best possible player flush (my K♥ blocks any higher hole heart). Only full houses or quads can beat me. Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, then check‑call on flop and turn) is consistent with Ax, medium pairs or single‑heart/connector holdings; if he had a full house he would often have raised the turn or would be polar‑shoving now. With full houses reasonably uncommon and a lot of worse hands (A‑x trips, smaller pairs, missed overcards) that will still call a river bet, I should extract value.

Sizing: pot is 9,800 — I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (6,500) to charge worse hands and deny a cheap showdown to marginal holdings. If Claude check‑raises large/all‑in, that’s a strong indicator of a full house and I’ll release; if he just calls I’m comfortably ahead with the nut flush.

Folding QcJc on river against Minni's 6,500 bet. I have queen-high with no made hand on a paired Ace board. Minni has shown consistent strength (bet flop, bet turn heavily when second Ace came, now betting river). My equity is approximately 10-15% at best, well below the 22.2% break-even threshold required by 3.5:1 pot odds. With 9 hands remaining, preserving chips and avoiding speculative calls with no showdown value is the correct approach. Minni's range is heavily weighted toward Aces, pairs, or completed draws - all of which beat my queen-high.

Raising to 250 with Q♥4♠ from the button in heads-up play. This is a standard aggressive line with a marginal hand that has reasonable fold equity against Minni. We're nearing the end of the tournament (hand 142 of 150), and I'm short-stacked relative to my opponent. Preflop aggression is essential to maintain chip position. The raise applies pressure while keeping stack commitment controlled. If Minni folds, I win the pot; if he calls, I have position postflop to make further decisions.

Call to 250 total. Heads-up vs a button open to 250, A♠6♥ is strong enough to defend — it has good equity against a wide opening range and blocks some strong A combos. Pot is 350; calling 150 more (250 total committed) gives correct pot odds (~30%) and keeps the pot manageable while out of position. A small 3-bet is a playable alternative, but it bloats the pot and often gets called by worse or 4-bet by better hands; I prefer to see a flop and play postflop with the nut-A potential and backdoor draws. Postflop plan: be aggressive when I pair the A or hit a favorable draw; on dry boards play cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy pressure).

Out of position with A♠6♥ on 5♦ 3♣ 5♥. I called preflop to see a flop and this is a very dry, paired low board that favors the preflop raiser’s range (pocket fives, other pocket pairs, overcards, some combos of 3s/5s). I have no pair and only a very small backdoor straight possibility; my Ace blocks some of the stronger A‑x combos but my showdown equity is modest. With the big chip advantage (28.7k vs 10.8k) I should avoid bloating the pot with a thin c‑bet/bluff out of position. Pot control and fold equity preservation are key — check and evaluate opponent’s action. Plan: check-call small to medium bets to realize equity, fold to large committed bets, and only start betting/raising when I actually pair the A or pick up clear fold equity or nut draws. Therefore: check.

Short-stacked endgame spot (hand 142/150). I raised preflop with Q♥4♠ and Minni called from BB. His flop check on a dry, paired board (5♦3♣5♥) indicates weakness. I have position and need to be aggressive to build chips with limited hands remaining. A bet of 300 applies reasonable pressure, is sized to win many pots immediately through fold equity, and controls commitment with my marginal hand. Even if called, I have two cards to come with some outs (any Queen). The dry board texture and Minni's check make this a profitable aggressive spot.

Out of position on a dry, paired flop (5♦-3♣-5♥) with A♠6♥. Claude (BTN) made a small c‑bet of 300 into a pot of 800 — if I call I risk 300 to win 1,400, so break‑even equity is ~21%. My hand blocks many A‑x combos, has ~6 outs to pair by river (combined turn+river equity ≈22%) and performs adequately against a wide BTN c‑bet range. Raising OOP with no pair or real draws would bloat the pot and often get me in trouble; folding for this price is too nitty. I will call to realize equity, keep worse hands and floats in, and re‑evaluate on the turn: bet/raise if I pair or pick up clear fold equity, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve.

Board is 5♦-3♣-5♥-3♠. My A♠6♥ now plays as the best possible kicker on the two-pair board (5s and 3s) unless Claude holds a 3 or a 5 (which would give him a full house/quads). Claude bet the flop (300) but checked the turn — that line lowers the probability he has a 3/5 full house (he could be pot-controlling or trapping, but checking the turn after betting the flop is more often weakness or medium strength). With the big stack advantage and the fact my Ace is a clean fifth card that beats most of his non-3/5 range, I want to extract value and deny a free river card. I’ll make a small-to-medium value bet (≈35% pot) to get called by A‑x (when he doesn’t have an Ace it’s worse), Kx, Qx and medium pairs that don’t contain a 3/5, while keeping the size manageable vs a potential check-raise. If Claude raises big (representing a full house/quads), I will fold — he’s very likely to have a locking hand. If he calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously (value bet thin if the river is a blank and he’s shown weakness; fold to big aggression if a 3/5 pairs the board). Bet 400 into the 1,100 pot.

Folding Q♥4♠ against Minni's check-raise on a double-paired board. My queen-high has minimal equity (~10-15%) against his range of trips or strong hands. While pot odds are somewhat favorable at 3.75:1, this is exactly the type of marginal situation where I should fold in the endgame with limited hands remaining (8 left). Preserving the 10,510 stack for better spots is higher EV than calling with such weak equity. Minni's line (check-flop, raise-turn) clearly indicates strength, and there's no reason to get involved here.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♠4♦. Against a single opponent this is a reasonable open — I want the initiative and position postflop. The pot is 150 and the call is 50, so I’ll min-raise to 200 (2x BB) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable given my weak kicker. Claude has ~10,410 (≈104 BB) so he can 3‑bet, but I can fold to large re-raises; if he calls I’ll C-bet on dry king/ace-high flops and play cautiously on coordinated or flushy boards. Overall this size balances fold equity and postflop flexibility to maximize chips over the remaining hands.

With Q2o in the big blind against Minni's button raise, this is a clear fold despite the 3:1 pot odds. Queen-deuce offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker - it has poor showdown value and limited connectivity. I'm also out of position after the flop, which compounds the weakness. While I'm in a shorter stack situation (10,410 vs Minni's 29,290) with only 7 hands remaining, I need to preserve chips for premium spots where I have better equity. Q2o doesn't meet that threshold. I should wait for stronger holdings like broadway cards, pairs, or better broadway combinations to make my move.

Folding 5-10 in the closing stages with a short stack. This is a weak hand that will struggle post-flop, especially out of position. With 6 hands remaining, I should preserve my 10,360 chip stack and wait for premium holdings where I can accumulate chips more effectively. 5-10 cannot withstand much post-flop pressure from an aggressive opponent like Minni.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♥8♣ — a strong, playable hand heads-up that benefits from initiative and position postflop. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call; min-raise is 200. I want to play aggressively to steal the BB and build the pot with a hand that plays well in position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size. Raise size 300 gives good fold equity against Claude’s defending/call-heavy tendencies and keeps weaker hands in to extract value postflop. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size, but for now take the initiative with a raise to 300.

Folding Qs2d from the big blind. This is a very weak hand, I'm out of position, and with only 6 hands remaining and a chip disadvantage (10,260 vs 29,340), I should preserve chips for better opportunities where I have stronger equity. The 200 additional chips are better saved for premium hands where I can make a more strategic stand.

Fold 2-10 offsuit. While position and cheap entry are attractive, with only 4 hands left in the tournament and a short stack of 10,210 chips, I should be more selective. This hand is too weak to justify the commitment even at low cost. Waiting for premium hands (pairs, broadway, AX) in the final hands maximizes win probability.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range to exploit fold equity. 2♥4♦ is a crappy hand for multiway play but acceptable as a steal heads-up. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to act; rather than limp, I’ll raise to a standard ~3x BB (to 300) to pressure Claude, deny a cheap flop and take the initiative. The size is small relative to my 29.7k stack but large enough to generate meaningful fold equity against Claude’s 10.1k stack. If he folds I take down the pot; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position and proceed cautiously; if he 3-bets large I’ll concede and fold to preserve my stack. Raise to 300.

Q4o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant getting involved. Against Minni's button raise, I'm out of position with minimal equity (roughly 25-30%), which falls below the 33% needed to justify the 2:1 pot odds. With only 4 hands remaining and a short stack, I should preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings where I have a clear advantage. Folding is the mathematically sound play here.

Folding T5o on the button with only 2 hands remaining in the tournament. While I'm getting 4:1 odds to call, T5o is too weak to build a pot with against an aggressive opponent. With a chip deficit, I should preserve my stack for stronger holdings in the final hand where I'll have position as well. Minni takes the small blind unchallenged.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with pocket 5s (5c5s). Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call. In heads-up play a raise is standard — I want to seize initiative, build a pot I can win with position, and keep worse hands and broadways in my caller range. A raise to 200 (2x BB) is a small, standard sizing that maximizes calls from worse hands while still applying pressure. Postflop I’ll c-bet on favorable textures, control the pot if I miss, and look to extract value when I hit a set. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (usually fold to very large 3-bets vs this stack), but call reasonable 3-bets to see flops. Therefore: raise to 200.

Calling the 100-chip raise from the Big Blind with 6-9 offsuit. The 3:1 pot odds are compelling, and I have reasonable equity against Minni's likely wide raising range from the button. With only one hand left after this, taking favorable odds is the right play. 6-9 has straight-making potential and reasonable playability post-flop.

With 6-9 offsuit on an Ah-Qh-Qs flop, I have a weak hand with only a gutshot straight draw (4 outs). This dangerous board likely connected with Minni's raising range from the button. I should check to keep the pot small and see what Minni does. Attempting a bluff with marginal equity is not profitable here. Checking allows me to evaluate Minni's betting pattern and make a better decision on subsequent streets while preserving chips for the final hand.

Flop Ah Qh Qs with 5♣5♠: I have a small pocket pair with two overcards and two queens on board. Claude’s BB calling range vs my raise contains lots of A‑x, Q‑x and heart combos — many of which will call a c‑bet or have nut/near‑nut holdings. There’s very little fold equity for a bluff and betting will often build a pot I’m a huge underdog in; with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with just a pair of fives. I’ll check to control the pot, see how Claude reacts, and fold to sizable aggression unless I improve or pick up a clear read.

Analyzing the turn action with 6c 9h on a board of Ah Qh Qs 6h:

Current hand strength: I have two pair - Queens (from the board) and Sixes (6h on board + 6c in my hand) with an Ace kicker. This is a strong made hand.

Key observations:
1. The board paired on the turn (6h) after both players checked the flop
2. Minni checked the flop on a dangerous A-Q-Q board, suggesting they either missed or have a marginal hand
3. I also have four hearts (Ah, Qh, 6h, 9h), so any heart on the river gives me a flush
4. The pot is only 400 chips; my stack of 9860 is substantial

Equity analysis:
- My two pair is strong against most hands except sets, full houses, or straights
- Given Minni checked the flop with A-Q-Q showing, they likely don't have premium holdings
- A sixth, Queen, or heart on the river dramatically improves my hand

Strategic decision:
Checking here is the right play because:
1. I get a free river card with strong potential (multiple outs for flushes, full houses, or the board to check through)
2. This can induce a bluff from Minni if they missed (both players checked the flop, suggesting modest holdings)
3. It keeps the pot small while I have strong equity
4. I still have hand 150 coming, so I don't need to overcommit now
5. Playing straightforwardly with my two pair is profitable - I'll let Minni determine the action

Checking also provides information: if Minni bets on the river with a weak-looking board, they may be bluffing or have missed, which I can then evaluate.

Heads‑up on the button with 5♣5♠ on A♥ Q♥ Q♠ 6♥. I only have a small pocket pair with no hearts — the board heavily favours Claude’s continuing calling range (A‑x, Q‑x, heart combos, sets). Claude checked flop and checked the turn, so there's little fold equity and betting here would simply build a pot I am a large dog in and will get called by better hands. I want to keep the pot controllable and use position to evaluate the river, so I check. Plan: if Claude bets the river I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to sizable aggression unless I improve or get a clear read); if checked through I can consider a thin river value bet or a targeted bluff depending on the river card.

Betting the river with two pair. Minni's flop check on a scary Ah-Qh-Qs board, followed by turn check after the 6h, indicates they likely have a marginal holding or complete miss. My two pair is a strong made hand that should extract value. A 300-chip bet builds the pot while remaining reasonable in sizing - enough to get called by weaker hands but not so large it looks like an overcommitment. This maximizes value from my strong position.

River: A♥ Q♥ Q♠ 6♥ 9♦; I hold 5♣5♠. Claude checked flop and turn and now bets a small 300 into a 700 pot. Calling 300 gives ~30% pot‑odds (300 to win 1000) so I only need ~30% equity to make a call profitable. Villain’s preflop calling range is wide (Ax, Kx, pocket pairs, some hearts, occasional bluffs) — many hands (A‑x, Q‑x, made hearts, big pairs) beat me, but a sizeable portion of his range (Kx, Jx, missed broadways, small pairs that I beat) will get to showdown and I’m ahead. The bet is small and looks like a thin value/block or a probe rather than huge polarization, so folding outright surrenders too much; bluff‑raising is unnecessary and risky given stack dynamics and the min‑raise size. With a big stack lead and a cheap price to see showdown, the lowest‑variance, +EV play is to call and see the showdown. If he were to raise big over this I’d re‑evaluate (likely fold). Therefore: call.

Raising to 250 (3x the big blind equivalent) applies appropriate pressure from the button in the final hand. Q6o is marginal, so I'm raising for fold equity and positional control rather than hand strength. This aggressive button raise forces Minni to make a decision with a wider range, and if called, I maintain position. The raise size is neither too small (which looks weak) nor too large (which commits me unnecessarily). In tournament poker's final moments, controlling the action from position with marginal hands is profitable strategy.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with K♠2♦. Claude opened from the button to 250; pot is 350 and it’s 150 to me. K2o is marginal and will often be dominated by the button’s opening range, and I’m out of position postflop, so a 3-bet here would be a thin, spewy bluff. Calling 150 (250 total committed including my BB) is cheap vs the pot and preserves my large stack while realizing equity. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously OOP — continue with aggression only when I improve or get a clear fold equity line, otherwise check/fold to significant action.

Check. I'm out of position with K♠2♦ on 8♦-4♥-A♥ — no pair, no heart/flush draw, and I'm very often behind vs the button's opening range (lots of A-x and overcards). Donk-betting OOP would be a thin, spewy bluff and susceptible to a raise or shove; better to control the pot and let Claude prove it. Plan: check and fold to large continuation bets, call small probes to gather info and see a turn, and only apply aggressive lines if I improve or get a clear fold equity spot.

Checking Q6o on an 8-4-A flop. I'm holding the weakest possible queen with no draws or pairs, and this board heavily favors the range that called my pre-flop raise. Minni's flop check suggests weakness, but my hand is too marginal to bluff profitably. Checking allows me to see the turn cheaply while maintaining the option to act if Minni bets or if I catch improvement. With a relatively short stack in the final hand, prudent pot control with a marginal holding is the correct strategy rather than forcing the action with air.

Turn: 8♦-4♥-A♥-9♠. I’m out of position with K♠2♦ — no pair, no heart/flush draw and very little showdown equity vs the button’s opening range (many A‑x and overcards). Pot is only 500 and there’s no bet to call; leading here would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called by A‑x or pressured by a raise. With a big stack advantage there’s no need to bloat the pot with air. I’ll control the pot size, check and fold to meaningful aggression, and only continue if Claude gives me a cheap look or I pick up a better bluff/value spot on the river.

With Q6o on 8-4-A-9, I have complete air (queen high with a weak kicker) and no draws. Minni's check on the flop and turn suggests weakness, but my hand is too marginal to bluff profitably at this stage. Checking preserves chips by avoiding a value-losing bluff into a hand range I likely cannot beat. This allows me to see the river while controlling downside risk on the final hand of the tournament.

The river paired my king, so I now have top pair (K♠2♦) but a very weak kicker. Claude opened from the button and then checked flop and turn — that line is consistent with either slowplayed A‑x, medium/weak hands he wants to control, or complete air. Betting here is thin: a small value bet will be called by some worse Kx/Qx hands but will also be called or raised by many A‑x that dominate me, so I’d be risking chips for little extra value. With a healthy stack and no need to force action, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and take the showdown. I’ll check and show; be prepared to fold to any unlikely large river raise since many of Claude’s checked hands beat me.

On the final river of the tournament, I hold Q6o on an 8-4-A-9-K board—complete air with no showdown value. Minni's river check indicates weakness, but bluffing is counterproductive: my pre-flop raise plus check-through line reads like a mediocre hand, a river bet screams desperation, and Minni's chip advantage (3:1) eliminates realistic fold equity. Checking allows me to see the showdown while preserving chips. With no draw, no pair, and a hopeless holding, running out the final hand with a check is the prudent decision.
